# Section 1: Introduction

1
00:00:00,210 --> 00:00:06,030
What is Utrillo, what can you do with it, and also what kind of art in the world are we going to use?

2
00:00:06,360 --> 00:00:08,370
There is simply but how do we know?

3
00:00:08,370 --> 00:00:15,780
Combines hardware with software in a perfect way for beginners to start creating hardware projects and

4
00:00:15,780 --> 00:00:18,050
control them with simple programming.

5
00:00:18,420 --> 00:00:23,040
And before we even go further, what can you do with encountering a bolt?

6
00:00:23,340 --> 00:00:25,520
Well, actually, a lot of things.

7
00:00:26,040 --> 00:00:30,600
Two of the most common project fields are home automation and the robotics.

8
00:00:30,930 --> 00:00:36,810
For example, with Arduino, you can create an alarm system in your home, control the movement of a

9
00:00:36,810 --> 00:00:45,330
robot, detect obstacles, make some music, create a weather station, work with Iot Internet of things,

10
00:00:45,600 --> 00:00:47,210
etc, etc..

11
00:00:47,580 --> 00:00:53,790
And our inner world is a small electronic board which contains what is called a microcontroller.

12
00:00:54,300 --> 00:00:59,550
The microcontroller will be responsible for executing the programs that you have written.

13
00:00:59,850 --> 00:01:06,840
Not that a microcontroller is quite different from what runs typical computers, which are microprocessors

14
00:01:07,440 --> 00:01:08,520
to give you inside.

15
00:01:08,670 --> 00:01:16,200
The microcontroller on the untrainable is running at 16 megahertz and has two kilobytes of memory on

16
00:01:16,200 --> 00:01:17,400
a standard computer.

17
00:01:17,550 --> 00:01:23,460
You can get, for example, a multicore two gigahertz c.p.u and eight gigabytes of RAM.

18
00:01:23,940 --> 00:01:27,700
When you look at the numbers, well, the difference is huge.

19
00:01:28,260 --> 00:01:34,500
So with a microcontroller and arduino, you can see that you won't be able to do things that require

20
00:01:34,500 --> 00:01:39,180
a lot of computation, power, for example, processing images.

21
00:01:39,540 --> 00:01:46,980
But on the other hand, a microcontroller is much cheaper than a typical laptop and is also much closer

22
00:01:46,980 --> 00:01:47,720
to hardware.

23
00:01:48,240 --> 00:01:54,480
You will see that with Arduino you can easily control many hardware components and the computation power

24
00:01:54,510 --> 00:01:57,330
is in fact good enough to run many projects.

25
00:01:57,570 --> 00:01:59,220
So don't worry about that.

26
00:01:59,580 --> 00:02:05,490
You just have to know that computers and electronic bolts such as the original are different things

27
00:02:05,700 --> 00:02:08,220
made for different applications.

28
00:02:08,610 --> 00:02:12,540
Now, where can you find microcontrollers in everyday life?

29
00:02:12,840 --> 00:02:15,180
Well, they are everywhere.

30
00:02:15,540 --> 00:02:22,140
Every day you see or interact with many electronic devices that are run by microcontrollers.

31
00:02:22,440 --> 00:02:29,160
For example, you can find microcontrollers in your phone, a microwave, a washing machine, a car,

32
00:02:29,280 --> 00:02:31,500
plane, etc., etc..

33
00:02:31,950 --> 00:02:37,270
Basically, any electronic machine contains one of many microcontrollers.

34
00:02:37,710 --> 00:02:45,750
Now, the thing is that working with electronics software and microcontrollers in general is quite difficult.

35
00:02:45,960 --> 00:02:49,670
And there is a big learning curve before you can do anything.

36
00:02:49,980 --> 00:02:51,810
And here comes Arduino.

37
00:02:52,230 --> 00:02:58,140
Thanks to our digital and the original boards, it is now much easier to start working with electronics

38
00:02:58,140 --> 00:02:59,490
and microcontrollers.

39
00:02:59,980 --> 00:03:05,640
Adreno is the easiest way to get to Felsted in the microcontroller and the electronics world.

40
00:03:06,180 --> 00:03:07,170
We've been out in the world.

41
00:03:07,200 --> 00:03:10,950
As you will see in this class, you can create fun and interesting.

42
00:03:10,950 --> 00:03:12,800
How do we project in no time?

43
00:03:13,230 --> 00:03:18,780
And after learning how to know if you want, you can dive deeper into the microcontroller world and

44
00:03:18,780 --> 00:03:24,870
reach a more advanced level which can lead you to more projects and for example, a great job.

45
00:03:25,320 --> 00:03:34,530
So with Ardino no complicated set up at all, just install the software that is called the Arduino Integrated

46
00:03:34,530 --> 00:03:37,290
Development Environment, and that's it.

47
00:03:37,530 --> 00:03:41,990
Then you can directly create circuits and upload programs to your bill.

48
00:03:42,540 --> 00:03:45,470
I will explain all that step by step in this course.

49
00:03:45,990 --> 00:03:48,200
Now, when was Outliner created?

50
00:03:48,690 --> 00:03:55,200
Well, first, when we speak about the name Arduino, we speak about the Arduino project initiated in

51
00:03:55,200 --> 00:04:02,520
2003 with the goal of providing a Low-Cost and easy way for any people to start hardware projects with

52
00:04:02,520 --> 00:04:03,630
microcontrollers.

53
00:04:04,170 --> 00:04:10,860
The first created board was named Arduino Uno and is still currently the most popular to get started

54
00:04:10,860 --> 00:04:15,510
with aluminum after so many other aluminum boards were created.

55
00:04:15,780 --> 00:04:20,220
And today you can still regularly see Newbold's that are released.

56
00:04:20,820 --> 00:04:27,240
So among the most commonly used balls, you can, for example, find the Arduino Nano, which is basically

57
00:04:27,240 --> 00:04:30,600
the same as we know that running on a much smaller board.

58
00:04:31,170 --> 00:04:37,680
You also have the Arduino Mir, which contains more hardware connectors and is a little bit more powerful.

59
00:04:38,240 --> 00:04:39,110
How do we know who know?

60
00:04:39,150 --> 00:04:45,810
Nano and Mir are the three most common boards you will find in many products, and then you have so

61
00:04:45,810 --> 00:04:50,940
many other bolts which contain specific functionalities for specific applications.

62
00:04:51,180 --> 00:04:54,300
So with all those bolts, why out you?

63
00:04:54,900 --> 00:04:57,030
Well, this is the most popular one.

64
00:04:57,330 --> 00:04:59,940
Easier to start and learn with and also with.

65
00:05:00,030 --> 00:05:07,080
And how do I make connections to start many projects, if you already have an amiable note that this

66
00:05:07,080 --> 00:05:12,780
is totally fine and this will work the same for you, and after this, if you have a different project

67
00:05:12,780 --> 00:05:19,220
and you find that another Arduino board would be more appropriate, then go ahead and get a new one.

68
00:05:19,890 --> 00:05:23,310
The original hardware and software are open source.

69
00:05:23,520 --> 00:05:30,660
You can find them on the Internet, for example, on websites such as GitHub and behind such a big open

70
00:05:30,660 --> 00:05:31,480
source project.

71
00:05:31,800 --> 00:05:34,930
You then also have a big open source community.

72
00:05:35,520 --> 00:05:39,380
This aspect is what makes Arduino great for your projects.

73
00:05:39,750 --> 00:05:42,260
You can easily share any project you have.

74
00:05:42,570 --> 00:05:49,380
Get feedback from more experienced users participating in other people's project, learn and discuss

75
00:05:49,380 --> 00:05:56,400
with many other people and find that we know is the most popular means that you would encounter even

76
00:05:56,400 --> 00:06:04,050
more projects and help using this board on the Internet to finish with the Arduino bode well.

77
00:06:04,110 --> 00:06:06,620
What can you actually find on that board?

78
00:06:07,140 --> 00:06:11,120
The first and most important thing is the microcontroller here.

79
00:06:11,610 --> 00:06:17,550
This way to run your code on the board and interact with the different hardware components you add to

80
00:06:17,550 --> 00:06:18,300
your secret.

81
00:06:18,930 --> 00:06:24,740
Then you have a USB port so that you can put it on and connect to the board, to your computer.

82
00:06:25,350 --> 00:06:27,540
You also get a DC Poljac.

83
00:06:27,540 --> 00:06:33,600
If you need external power supply, you should also have a push button on the board, which is named

84
00:06:33,600 --> 00:06:37,110
the reset button to restart your program.

85
00:06:37,650 --> 00:06:43,350
We will talk about this maldito indicus directly on the board that you have all the components to make

86
00:06:43,350 --> 00:06:48,900
the board run correctly, but you don't need to worry about those phone because that's not really important

87
00:06:48,900 --> 00:06:49,740
to get started.

88
00:06:50,280 --> 00:06:56,670
And last but not least, the hardware connectors to plug different hardware components on the Arduino,

89
00:06:57,030 --> 00:07:02,610
you get 14 digital things, six unappeased and some power pins.

90
00:07:02,880 --> 00:07:06,120
We're going to use those pins extensively during the course.

91
00:07:06,390 --> 00:07:11,130
And then I'm going to explain to you everything to know about them in the following sections.

92
00:07:11,580 --> 00:07:12,060
All right.

93
00:07:12,150 --> 00:07:13,740
That's pretty much it for the board.

94
00:07:14,100 --> 00:07:20,550
Notes that because Arduino is an open source project with open source hardware, then you can actually

95
00:07:20,550 --> 00:07:25,590
find many different Arduino boards produced by different manufacturers.

96
00:07:25,980 --> 00:07:32,910
Depending on the board, the color and layout may be different, but the pin layout and microcontroller

97
00:07:32,910 --> 00:07:33,750
don't change.

98
00:07:34,020 --> 00:07:36,450
So basically nothing changes for you.

99
00:07:37,050 --> 00:07:37,560
All right.

100
00:07:37,560 --> 00:07:44,010
Now, you should have a better idea of what Arduino is and is, not what you can do with it and what

101

102

OK.

for this course.

51
00:02:57,600 --> 00:02:58,140
All right.

52
00:02:58,140 --> 00:03:03,450
And with this setup, so not much to do, you are good to go with the version two of the Arduino IDE.

53
00:03:03,570 --> 00:03:10,920
You can skip the rest of the video unless you are using instead the version one of the Arduino IDE.

54
00:03:13,230 --> 00:03:17,070
So first open the original ID and you might notice.

55
00:03:17,070 --> 00:03:22,020
Then the text may be quite small for you to read.

56
00:03:22,320 --> 00:03:27,870
And when you program, it's actually better to be comfortable with the size of text.

57
00:03:28,350 --> 00:03:38,100
So what you can do first is price control or command and use the scroll of the mouse to zoom in and

58
00:03:38,100 --> 00:03:38,820
zoom out.

59
00:03:39,000 --> 00:03:45,120
And this way you can actually make the size of the text bigger.

60
00:03:45,750 --> 00:03:54,360
If you want a more permanent solution, you can go here on file, open preferences, and here you can

61
00:03:54,360 --> 00:03:55,860
see the font size.

62
00:03:55,860 --> 00:04:04,020
So 15 was the one I just have here with the zoom with the mouse and you can choose any font you want.

63
00:04:04,350 --> 00:04:14,720
I'm going to put 18, for example, and I'm going also to scale in the face a little bit to 130%.

64
00:04:14,730 --> 00:04:16,110
You don't need to change that.

65
00:04:16,110 --> 00:04:16,360
Okay.

66
00:04:16,380 --> 00:04:21,300
This will make, for example, the green buttons here bigger.

67
00:04:21,420 --> 00:04:28,770
But what you can do is keep this automatic and change the font size with something that you are comfortable

68
00:04:28,770 --> 00:04:29,220
with.

69
00:04:29,610 --> 00:04:33,650
It may be 18, it may be 16 or it may be 22.

70
00:04:33,660 --> 00:04:35,740
Whatever makes you more comfortable.

71
00:04:35,760 --> 00:04:38,190
I'm going to click on okay.

72
00:04:38,640 --> 00:04:42,150
And actually, I'm going to restart the adenoid.

73
00:04:47,800 --> 00:04:50,530
Okay, so now it's actually much better.

74
00:04:50,950 --> 00:04:55,900
One thing we can do also is we go back to preferences.

75
00:04:55,990 --> 00:04:58,180
We can put line number.

76
00:04:58,180 --> 00:05:02,560
So display line number, you can check this option click on.

77
00:05:02,560 --> 00:05:03,100
Okay.

78
00:05:03,100 --> 00:05:05,380
And now you have the line numbers here.

79
00:05:05,410 --> 00:05:11,110
This will make it easier for you when you need to communicate with other people about your code.

80
00:05:11,350 --> 00:05:15,680
And your other program will be a little bit more readable.

81
00:05:15,700 --> 00:05:23,140
And now the last thing I'm going to do is to actually change the font for the text, because as you

82
00:05:23,140 --> 00:05:29,400
can see, maybe the font here is not the best one for readability purpose.

83
00:05:29,410 --> 00:05:37,270
So what I'm going to do is go on file preferences again, and here you can directly change the font

84
00:05:37,450 --> 00:05:38,950
on this menu.

85
00:05:38,950 --> 00:05:41,890
You need to go to this file here.

86
00:05:41,890 --> 00:05:46,030
So on the bottom you should have a file this will you click on this?

87
00:05:46,030 --> 00:05:54,760
This will open a new file manager and what I'm going to do so I'm going to close the Arduino ID now

88
00:05:54,760 --> 00:06:01,030
we are on this file manager here and I'm going to click on preferences that txt.

89
00:06:01,030 --> 00:06:05,350
So I'm going to open it and I'm going to search for font.

90
00:06:05,350 --> 00:06:10,330
So a little dot font here we just found it.

91
00:06:10,330 --> 00:06:15,340
And the font, the basic one is mono spaced here on windows.

92
00:06:15,610 --> 00:06:18,640
And then you have, as you can see, the size of the font.

93
00:06:18,640 --> 00:06:24,490
And what I'm going to do is I'm going to change mono spaced to console us.

94
00:06:24,490 --> 00:06:27,970
So console us.

95
00:06:28,330 --> 00:06:30,160
This will be the font I will use.

96
00:06:30,160 --> 00:06:33,700
And if you want, you can also change and see if it's better for you.

97
00:06:33,880 --> 00:06:35,950
And it's very important.

98
00:06:35,980 --> 00:06:42,110
Note that you should first quit the Arduino IDE and then edits the file.

99
00:06:42,310 --> 00:06:50,860
Don't edit the file while the Arduino ID is still running so I can save the file, save preferences

100
00:06:50,860 --> 00:06:55,810
the txt and now I can start Arduino again.

101
00:06:56,170 --> 00:07:02,530
And as you can see, the font here is actually better for readability.

102
00:07:02,950 --> 00:07:04,480
And one quick note here.

103
00:07:04,660 --> 00:07:10,690
Actually, here you can see the font and then plain you may want to change it to bold, but actually

104
00:07:10,690 --> 00:07:17,680
this option doesn't work for now with the albino ID, so you can just change the font here and then

105
00:07:17,680 --> 00:07:18,910
the size.

106
00:07:19,150 --> 00:07:19,690
All right.

107
00:07:19,690 --> 00:07:23,380
With this setup, you are good to go in the following of the course.

108
00:07:23,380 --> 00:07:30,970
At some point I will come back to the different options and tools you can get with the Arduino IDE and

109
00:07:30,970 --> 00:07:32,980
you will learn how to use them properly.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,270 --> 00:00:00,660
Right.

2
00:00:00,660 --> 00:00:05,660
You now have the original ID installed and set up on your computer.

3
00:00:05,670 --> 00:00:10,340
Now take your Arduino board and the USB cable, which goes with it.

4
00:00:10,350 --> 00:00:15,150
Simply plug your Arduino to your computer using the cable.

5
00:00:21,200 --> 00:00:23,060
And now how to make your Arduino.

6
00:00:23,060 --> 00:00:27,430
ID know that your Arduino board is connected to your computer.

7
00:00:27,440 --> 00:00:33,480
Well, you can go on tools menu here and you will need to check two things first.

8
00:00:33,500 --> 00:00:34,760
Here board.

9
00:00:34,760 --> 00:00:38,750
So tools and board, you will have different kind of board.

10
00:00:38,780 --> 00:00:43,880
So for example, Arduino, UNO, Arduino, Nano, Mega, Leonardo, etc..

11
00:00:44,000 --> 00:00:51,530
So if you are using an Arduino UNO like I'm using now, make sure that Arduino UNO is checked here.

12
00:00:52,370 --> 00:00:54,110
This is the first step.

13
00:00:54,350 --> 00:00:58,780
And then you will have here to check the port.

14
00:00:58,790 --> 00:01:06,890
So tools, ports and make sure that the correct port for the correct board is selected.

15
00:01:06,920 --> 00:01:13,740
You may have multiple ports here and you maybe don't know which one corresponds to your Arduino.

16
00:01:13,760 --> 00:01:16,910
So for me, it's already detected as an Arduino.

17
00:01:18,200 --> 00:01:23,210
But if you don't know which port is which, well, there is a simple way to know.

18
00:01:23,210 --> 00:01:25,070
You can so click here.

19
00:01:25,370 --> 00:01:31,600
You can n plug your Arduino board so disconnected from your computer.

20
00:01:31,610 --> 00:01:33,200
Then you go on tools.

21
00:01:34,040 --> 00:01:38,600
So you can see I don't have any port selected or maybe you will have different ones.

22
00:01:38,600 --> 00:01:44,420
For example, come one, come three, etc. and then you can plug your Arduino back.

23
00:01:45,430 --> 00:01:45,720
Okay.

24
00:01:45,730 --> 00:01:47,260
And go on tools again.

25
00:01:47,260 --> 00:01:50,260
And you should see now the pot is here.

26
00:01:50,920 --> 00:01:54,250
You can also click on here, get more info.

27
00:01:54,550 --> 00:01:57,790
This will tell you this should tell you depends on the board.

28
00:01:57,790 --> 00:02:00,010
But what kind of board do you have?

29
00:02:00,010 --> 00:02:02,800
So you are sure you don't make any mistake?

30
00:02:03,280 --> 00:02:05,350
And this is a basic program.

31
00:02:05,350 --> 00:02:08,080
So this is the minimum code for original program.

32
00:02:08,080 --> 00:02:10,300
This code does actually nothing.

33
00:02:10,300 --> 00:02:13,540
I'm going to explain all that later in the course.

34
00:02:13,870 --> 00:02:20,140
But for out what you can do once you are sure that wall and ports are correct here, you can click on

35
00:02:20,140 --> 00:02:20,860
verify.

36
00:02:20,860 --> 00:02:30,130
So this button, this will compile the code and you can see here the green bar down compiling and then

37
00:02:30,130 --> 00:02:38,110
you can click on the upload button here and this will compile and upload the code to your Arduino.

38
00:02:38,110 --> 00:02:45,220
And once you see done uploading, it means that this program is actually uploaded correctly to your

39
00:02:45,220 --> 00:02:46,810
physical Arduino board.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,270 --> 00:00:05,790
And now if you don't want to buy your natural abode or use any hardware component, here is how you

2
00:00:05,790 --> 00:00:09,270
can complete this course with just a simulation.

3
00:00:09,300 --> 00:00:16,500
Note that even if you have no board and components for the course, I still advise you to watch this

4
00:00:16,500 --> 00:00:19,350
lesson and get familiar with Tinker CAD.

5
00:00:19,530 --> 00:00:25,230
During the course I will sometimes use the Tinker CAD interface for explanations.

6
00:00:25,230 --> 00:00:33,150
And also it is nice for you to always have the possibility to test a programme on a simulation before

7
00:00:33,150 --> 00:00:35,400
doing it in real life.

8
00:00:35,490 --> 00:00:37,500
So what is Tinker CAD?

9
00:00:37,740 --> 00:00:46,500
Well, Tinker Cat is a free online tool already used by Autodesk, and you don't need to do any installation.

10
00:00:46,530 --> 00:00:55,020
Everything is happening in your web browser, so you can simply open a web browser and type tinker CAD

11
00:00:55,050 --> 00:01:01,530
on Google and click on the first link, which should be Tinker CAD dot com.

12
00:01:03,270 --> 00:01:03,690
All right.

13
00:01:03,690 --> 00:01:06,780
And this is the home page from Chincoteague.

14
00:01:06,870 --> 00:01:09,760
And you can go directly here.

15
00:01:09,780 --> 00:01:20,310
Join now and you can create a personal account so you can choose to sign in with Google, Apple, Microsoft,

16
00:01:20,310 --> 00:01:22,040
Facebook or email.

17
00:01:22,050 --> 00:01:25,710
So just sign up using whatever method you prefer.

18
00:01:26,400 --> 00:01:32,430
And once you have created your accounts, you can directly sign in and you will get to this interface,

19
00:01:32,430 --> 00:01:34,560
the Tinker Card dashboard.

20
00:01:34,560 --> 00:01:40,140
And on the left here, you will make sure to click on designs and then you can click on circuits.

21
00:01:40,320 --> 00:01:41,680
You can choose circuits here.

22
00:01:41,700 --> 00:01:43,260
So for you, it's going to be empty.

23
00:01:43,260 --> 00:01:47,680
But as you go and create new circuits, you will see all your circuits here.

24
00:01:47,700 --> 00:01:51,840
And so you can click on new and create a new circuit.

25
00:01:53,360 --> 00:01:56,380
And this will open a new page.

26
00:01:57,320 --> 00:02:02,940
This is kind of a workspace where you can build any secret you want.

27
00:02:02,960 --> 00:02:07,410
So the name here will be automatically generated.

28
00:02:07,430 --> 00:02:11,040
You can just change the name as you want.

29
00:02:11,060 --> 00:02:13,310
So here this will be, for example, test.

30
00:02:15,100 --> 00:02:22,720
And then so I'm going to zoom in a little bit then to build a circuit where you have all of the components

31
00:02:22,720 --> 00:02:28,420
you want here on the right so you can directly search for components.

32
00:02:28,420 --> 00:02:33,790
So for example, Arduino, you select Arduino, Arduino UNO three.

33
00:02:33,790 --> 00:02:35,980
So this is the board we want to use.

34
00:02:35,980 --> 00:02:40,540
So you can click on this board and put it here on the dashboard.

35
00:02:40,570 --> 00:02:43,780
You can click here to automatically Zoom.

36
00:02:43,780 --> 00:02:48,580
And if you use the scroll from the mouse, well, you can zoom in and zoom out.

37
00:02:48,790 --> 00:02:49,540
So great.

38
00:02:49,540 --> 00:02:52,870
Now you have your original world, which is a simulation.

39
00:02:52,870 --> 00:02:57,740
And now what we need is to actually be able to write some code for this board.

40
00:02:57,760 --> 00:03:05,590
So you have a here, you can click on this code button, this will open the code interface.

41
00:03:05,590 --> 00:03:10,780
And actually this is not text code, This is a block interface.

42
00:03:11,200 --> 00:03:15,160
And you are going to select here just text.

43
00:03:15,160 --> 00:03:15,360
Okay?

44
00:03:15,400 --> 00:03:17,530
We are going to write code directly.

45
00:03:18,040 --> 00:03:19,450
You have a warning.

46
00:03:19,450 --> 00:03:20,080
Are you sure?

47
00:03:20,080 --> 00:03:20,590
Yes.

48
00:03:20,590 --> 00:03:28,270
You click on continue and you have so you have an already existing program that we are going to see

49
00:03:28,270 --> 00:03:29,350
later, of course.

50
00:03:29,350 --> 00:03:36,400
And well, this is the same as writing code on the Arduino ID, so you can click on here code to toggle

51
00:03:36,400 --> 00:03:45,040
the code editor and here you can add any other component at any time if you want to, and if you want

52
00:03:45,040 --> 00:03:46,180
to start the simulation.

53
00:03:46,180 --> 00:03:53,800
So basically, if you want to transfer this code into this fake ball, you click on start simulation

54
00:03:54,310 --> 00:03:57,820
and now the program is inside the adeno board.

55
00:03:58,090 --> 00:03:59,740
So you don't need to set up anything.

56
00:03:59,740 --> 00:04:01,180
This is done automatically.

57
00:04:01,450 --> 00:04:06,220
And if you want to actually change the code here, I'll change the circuit.

58
00:04:06,220 --> 00:04:11,950
Well, you have to first stop the simulation so you can start the simulation.

59
00:04:12,190 --> 00:04:13,140
Stop this action.

60
00:04:13,180 --> 00:04:20,080
This is one difference with the Arduino ID is that once the code is running on your board in the original

61
00:04:20,110 --> 00:04:26,050
ID, you can always write some new code, but not on the simulation.

62
00:04:26,050 --> 00:04:29,770
You need to first stop the simulation and then change the code.

63
00:04:30,160 --> 00:04:30,580
All right.

64
00:04:30,580 --> 00:04:33,190
And as you can see, all change is saved.

65
00:04:33,190 --> 00:04:35,350
So this will be saved automatically.

66
00:04:35,350 --> 00:04:40,450
You can click on this Tinker Code button here to come back to your menu.

67
00:04:40,450 --> 00:04:44,950
And here you have your circuit so you can create a new one if you want.

68
00:04:44,950 --> 00:04:51,160
And you can just click here to go back to the previous circuit.

69
00:04:55,310 --> 00:04:55,790
All right.

70
00:04:55,790 --> 00:05:02,660
And now you are ready to create your first Arduino program, whether you use a physical Arduino board

71
00:05:02,660 --> 00:05:07,250
with a hardware component or the Tinker CAD simulation.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 3: Your First Arduino Project

1
00:00:00,180 --> 00:00:06,930
It's time to write your first out in a program, you know, have everything set up and ready that you

2
00:00:06,930 --> 00:00:08,550
use a real power in the world.

3
00:00:08,590 --> 00:00:14,940
We have no idea all these simulation ginko can in the next few lessons.

4
00:00:14,940 --> 00:00:19,680
You understand the basics on how our program works.

5
00:00:20,020 --> 00:00:27,640
You will create and upload your first program and also learn how to and restart an out in your program.

6
00:00:28,590 --> 00:00:30,840
No need for any other component yet.

7
00:00:31,260 --> 00:00:32,820
Just you are bored.

8
00:00:32,820 --> 00:00:39,270
And the USB cable will also provide instructions for the simulation on Tinker Card.

9
00:00:39,630 --> 00:00:46,140
If you are using the simulation, please make sure to watch all of the other lessons first in the audio

10
00:00:46,350 --> 00:00:50,310
and then the lesson on how to do the same with the simulation.

11
00:00:50,640 --> 00:00:51,590
Let's get started.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,150 --> 00:00:07,080
When you start your I.D., you indirectly get a new amnesty program, but you can see here, in fact,

2
00:00:07,080 --> 00:00:08,830
the program is not really empty.

3
00:00:09,150 --> 00:00:14,690
It already contains two functions void setup and void loop.

4
00:00:15,630 --> 00:00:17,980
If you don't know what functions are.

5
00:00:18,360 --> 00:00:22,090
Don't worry, I'm going to cover that a little bit later in the course.

6
00:00:22,770 --> 00:00:29,220
So for any original program you create, this is the minimum code you should have.

7
00:00:29,880 --> 00:00:38,490
If one of those functions is missing, you won't be able to compete in the code, do you, on a board.

8
00:00:38,940 --> 00:00:46,530
And when creating your own program, you will write some code inside each of those functions.

9
00:00:46,860 --> 00:00:50,630
This code will then be executed on the Arab world.

10
00:00:51,360 --> 00:00:55,500
So the first thing you can see is that the functions are empty.

11
00:00:55,700 --> 00:01:00,330
OK, so when you run the program, what will happen?

12
00:01:00,960 --> 00:01:07,920
So first of all, if you put any instruction here at the beginning of your program, this will be executed

13
00:01:08,190 --> 00:01:12,930
and then the void setup function will be executed first.

14
00:01:13,170 --> 00:01:23,130
So whatever code you put between those two curly brackets here will be executed first after it is executed

15
00:01:23,130 --> 00:01:25,140
once and only once.

16
00:01:25,740 --> 00:01:34,570
The code inside the void loop function will be executed in what we can call an infinite loop.

17
00:01:34,920 --> 00:01:41,730
So basically all the code here will be executed and once it is finished, it will be executed again

18
00:01:41,730 --> 00:01:43,860
and again and all of that again.

19
00:01:44,070 --> 00:01:51,450
So first, the code in the void setup executive wants and then the code in the void loop is executed

20
00:01:51,450 --> 00:02:00,870
an infinite number of time until you simply restart the program or you shut down your arduino and inside

21
00:02:00,880 --> 00:02:01,620
the function.

22
00:02:01,640 --> 00:02:07,290
So inside the void set up, for example, the instructions will be executed line by line.

23
00:02:07,320 --> 00:02:10,740
So first line five and then line six, etc..

24
00:02:11,100 --> 00:02:13,890
As you can see, we already have some text here.

25
00:02:13,890 --> 00:02:15,780
But this is not code.

26
00:02:16,110 --> 00:02:17,810
This is just a command.

27
00:02:18,030 --> 00:02:19,380
So we've double slash.

28
00:02:19,620 --> 00:02:23,140
You can actually create some command if output doubles.

29
00:02:23,730 --> 00:02:27,750
I can just create any comment I want.

30
00:02:28,080 --> 00:02:33,200
This is very useful for you to improve the credibility of your program.

31
00:02:33,210 --> 00:02:38,980
OK, so you can put some command to give some indication of what's happening in your code.

32
00:02:39,480 --> 00:02:45,680
OK, in the same folder, void loop function, all the lines will be executed one by one.

33
00:02:45,960 --> 00:02:48,480
And this is also a command line.

34
00:02:49,140 --> 00:02:54,330
So we're going to come back to those functions quite a few times in the schools.

35
00:02:54,630 --> 00:03:02,820
But basically what you would write inside the void setup function is the code to initialize your program,

36
00:03:03,030 --> 00:03:09,180
for example, to initialize valuable, to set up a sensor, to set up a communication.

37
00:03:09,540 --> 00:03:14,190
So everything that you need to set up at the beginning of your program and that you basically need to

38
00:03:14,190 --> 00:03:19,410
set up once and then in the loop function, you rewrite your application code.

39
00:03:19,590 --> 00:03:27,570
So that would be the code that we learn all over again until the admin board is powered off, all restarted.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,330 --> 00:00:03,540
Let's create your first real original program.

2
00:00:04,080 --> 00:00:08,850

# Section 4: Create an Arduino circuit

1
00:00:00,300 --> 00:00:04,130
Great, you have written and uploaded your first Arduino program.

2
00:00:04,590 --> 00:00:08,330
Now you are going to build your first secret, we breadboard.

3
00:00:09,060 --> 00:00:15,030
These is the true power of Arduino controlling hardware components to create fan applications.

4
00:00:15,600 --> 00:00:19,530
In the following lessons, I will first explain to you and breadboard works.

5
00:00:19,890 --> 00:00:25,470
Then you will see that for most of the components you will plug to your out a bold you will need to

6
00:00:25,470 --> 00:00:26,930
add a store.

7
00:00:27,270 --> 00:00:34,680
I will give you all the tools you need so you can easily find which resistor is which thanks to a color

8
00:00:34,680 --> 00:00:35,150
code.

9
00:00:35,460 --> 00:00:42,810
And then you will build your first circuit with an entity programmed to make ready do something.

10
00:00:43,200 --> 00:00:48,990
In the following of discourse, you will add more components to the circuits, making it more complex

11
00:00:49,230 --> 00:00:52,830
and with more functionalities for the explanations.

12
00:00:52,830 --> 00:01:00,270
I will use both the hardware and the Tinker CAD simulation so you will be able to build any secrets

13
00:01:00,270 --> 00:01:01,020
in the cloud.

14
00:01:01,500 --> 00:01:08,070
Also, building a circuit with this simulation might sometimes be a good idea if you just want to test

15
00:01:08,220 --> 00:01:11,990
something first before implementing it in real life.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,240 --> 00:00:06,750
The breadboard is a super useful tool to prototype any secrets with your Arduino without a breadboard,

2
00:00:06,900 --> 00:00:12,500
you would have to sodor all components in a secret so they can connect between each other.

3
00:00:12,720 --> 00:00:19,650
And then again, for each modification you make on the circuit, as you can guess, this would quickly

4
00:00:19,650 --> 00:00:22,040
be messy and unreliable.

5
00:00:22,350 --> 00:00:28,170
The breadboard allows you to connect all your components with your Arduino and between each other in

6
00:00:28,170 --> 00:00:30,150
an easy and practical way.

7
00:00:30,540 --> 00:00:34,580
Then modifying the circuit can be as short as a few seconds.

8
00:00:35,160 --> 00:00:37,560
So how does a bride both work?

9
00:00:38,020 --> 00:00:44,670
Let's have a look with the Tinker CAD simulation here and then I will show you the real physical Brainbow.

10
00:00:44,940 --> 00:00:52,220
So on, Dinko, you simply go on the component on the right here and you can start to search for Bridel.

11
00:00:52,890 --> 00:00:55,740
You have so you have different kinds of breadboard.

12
00:00:55,980 --> 00:01:01,950
Basically, they are all the same, can have a small one and breadboard.

13
00:01:01,980 --> 00:01:09,190
So this is the standard one standard size and this is half the size of a normal Brainbow.

14
00:01:09,780 --> 00:01:16,050
So as you can see, there are many holes here where you can connect wires and components.

15
00:01:16,470 --> 00:01:22,470
And what you need to know is that underneath the surface there are some metallic wires between some

16
00:01:22,470 --> 00:01:27,300
of the holes of the board which make them electrically connected.

17
00:01:28,580 --> 00:01:35,480
So the most important thing to learn here is how those bones are connected between each other and you

18
00:01:35,480 --> 00:01:39,320
will see it's actually quite simple and we're the dingo get simulation we get.

19
00:01:39,470 --> 00:01:42,630
And there is good and simple overview to understand that.

20
00:01:42,650 --> 00:01:47,990
So first of all, you can see that here you have two lines.

21
00:01:47,990 --> 00:01:51,540
So you have a plus and minus line or it can be red or blue.

22
00:01:51,920 --> 00:01:54,560
OK, you have the same on the small breadboard.

23
00:01:55,650 --> 00:02:02,680
And those two lines will be used for so the plus are the right one would be used for power supply and

24
00:02:02,740 --> 00:02:07,170
minus all the blue dark one will be used for the ground.

25
00:02:07,320 --> 00:02:13,560
So the ground of the secret is basically a common point between all of your components.

26
00:02:13,800 --> 00:02:15,300
And that's very important.

27
00:02:15,300 --> 00:02:19,580
So you can have a reference point for any voltage in your circuit.

28
00:02:19,980 --> 00:02:26,820
So as you can see here, I'm going to zoom a little bit when I choose one of the dots here.

29
00:02:27,450 --> 00:02:31,670
You can see all the dots here connected in the Green Line.

30
00:02:31,800 --> 00:02:32,980
So are the same.

31
00:02:33,300 --> 00:02:40,510
That's so if I connect something here, if I connect something here, that's the same.

32
00:02:40,770 --> 00:02:46,890
OK, so everything you connect on this line will be connected to all of the other components on this

33
00:02:46,890 --> 00:02:47,130
line.

34
00:02:47,460 --> 00:02:50,780
And the same happens for this line here.

35
00:02:51,060 --> 00:02:55,680
But as you can see, the first and the second line are independent.

36
00:02:56,130 --> 00:03:04,010
So one line foreground, one line for power supply and you have the same here, power supply and ground

37
00:03:04,140 --> 00:03:10,290
and on the small breadboard, that's the same ground power supply here and ground supply here.

38

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,180 --> 00:00:06,540
Before we go and manipulate real hardware components, here is a quick word of caution, though.

2
00:00:06,570 --> 00:00:12,030
In any case, you don't really risk anything for yourself or other persons.

3
00:00:12,390 --> 00:00:16,190
The current and voltage inside the Arduino are really not that high.

4
00:00:16,410 --> 00:00:23,580
The biggest risk you have is to make a short circuit which may damage hardware components all the board

5
00:00:23,580 --> 00:00:24,210
itself.

6
00:00:24,450 --> 00:00:26,730
In most cases, you should be pretty safe.

7
00:00:26,940 --> 00:00:33,180
The Arduino is actually quite hard to burn, but it's still possible if you manipulate it in the wrong

8
00:00:33,180 --> 00:00:33,510
way.

9
00:00:34,050 --> 00:00:38,340
Don't worry, though, if you follow a few basic rules, you will have no problem.

10
00:00:38,760 --> 00:00:45,240
First, when you want to modify something in the circuit, even just one wire, then if you are not

11
00:00:45,240 --> 00:00:53,040
100 percent sure of what you do, the best way is to first power of the original, which means removing

12
00:00:53,040 --> 00:00:57,420
the USB cable from the other side of the computer side.

13
00:00:57,810 --> 00:01:05,370
Make your modifications double, check your security and power the other again, second, double or

14
00:01:05,370 --> 00:01:05,850
triple.

15
00:01:05,850 --> 00:01:09,990
Check all your circuits before you power on the aluminum.

16
00:01:10,560 --> 00:01:13,500
This will make you avoid very simple mistakes.

17
00:01:13,710 --> 00:01:19,500
Like, for example, connecting five votes are a digital pin directly to the ground.

18
00:01:20,340 --> 00:01:25,930
I personally double check all my circuits, even for the most simple ones.

19
00:01:26,440 --> 00:01:36,570
Third, no Wendy Adriano Espaillat on don't use any metric tool like a screwdriver are open to touch

20
00:01:36,570 --> 00:01:37,320
the circuit.

21
00:01:37,710 --> 00:01:43,950
With this, you already have a chance of creating a short circuit and thus burn a component all the

22
00:01:43,950 --> 00:01:44,780
out in a boulder.

23
00:01:45,480 --> 00:01:51,900
And finally, for when you build a circuit, you will connect multiple hardware components together

24
00:01:51,930 --> 00:01:54,160
and all with the pins.

25
00:01:54,750 --> 00:01:59,860
Make sure that all your components are connected to the same common ground.

26
00:02:00,270 --> 00:02:05,850
This is super important both for safety and to get great results.

27
00:02:06,330 --> 00:02:11,760
A good practice is to first connect the ground whenever you add a new component.

28
00:02:12,180 --> 00:02:15,830
And we will see more about that in the following license, of course.

29
00:02:16,370 --> 00:02:16,890
All right.

30
00:02:16,890 --> 00:02:20,040
Now, don't be too scared of manipulating your in a board.

31
00:02:20,220 --> 00:02:24,480
If you follow those basic recommendations, you should be totally safe.

32
00:02:25,070 --> 00:02:30,480
The end of the course with a little bit more experience, you will be confident enough to build your

33
00:02:30,480 --> 00:02:35,040
own circuits and applications in a safe way.

34
00:02:35,250 --> 00:02:37,380
And now let's build that first secret.

00:03:55,770 --> 00:04:00,830
Not an integer in order is stored in two debates.

54
00:04:01,410 --> 00:04:10,260
OK, and what does it mean for you practically is that you can store any value from minus thirty two

55
00:04:10,260 --> 00:04:17,940
thousand seven hundred sixty eight two plus thirty two thousand seven hundred sixty seven, if you try

56
00:04:17,950 --> 00:04:20,290
to start with a value that is bigger than that.

57
00:04:20,770 --> 00:04:23,730
Well, this will what we call overflow.

58
00:04:24,130 --> 00:04:26,010
So let's say you want to store.

59
00:04:26,310 --> 00:04:27,290
Let's make an example.

60
00:04:27,300 --> 00:04:36,330
Actually, I'm going to talk thirty two thousand seven hundred sixty eight, which is just above the

61
00:04:36,330 --> 00:04:36,840
limit.

62
00:04:37,110 --> 00:04:38,940
And let's print a.

63
00:04:44,670 --> 00:04:51,300
And you can see it's not that at all, it's minus thirty two thousand seven hundred sixty eight, which

64
00:04:51,300 --> 00:04:52,800
is the other opposite.

65
00:04:53,400 --> 00:04:58,110
So whenever you go below that value, you will actually come back to the maximum.

66
00:04:58,350 --> 00:05:02,370
And whenever you go after that value, you will come back to the minimum.

67
00:05:02,670 --> 00:05:04,260
This is what we call other flu.

68
00:05:04,440 --> 00:05:09,690
And this is a pretty common area you will get in your programs if, for example, you don't understand

69
00:05:09,690 --> 00:05:12,910
why you get some weird values with your integer numbers.

70
00:05:13,110 --> 00:05:14,780
Well, that may be because of debt.

71
00:05:15,090 --> 00:05:19,420
So when you use integer, make sure you don't use integer for high values.

72
00:05:19,670 --> 00:05:25,020
So whenever you need to use something more than, let's say, 10 or 20 thousands and you are not sure

73
00:05:25,020 --> 00:05:29,610
that you know the maximum, well, integer is not the best data type.

74

How can you fix that?

75

Well, you can use the data type long and a long data type is actually stored on four bytes, which

76
00:05:40,320 --> 00:05:47,110
means that you can go from two billion something to minus two billion, two plus two million.

77
00:05:47,670 --> 00:05:50,970
So we've long you should not have really any program.

78
00:05:51,420 --> 00:05:54,690
And the way to use long is the same as integer.

80

So you declare and you initialize it.

81

I know if I print out here, this is four hundred thousand.

82
00:06:10,910 --> 00:06:13,790
Now, we should have the correct value.

83
00:06:13,860 --> 00:06:20,120
Yes, the four big integer numbers used long for smaller one use integer.

84

All right, so now you have a global overview of what variables and why we need them.

If you want to store, let's say, a user age, then simply name your valuable user age.

00:06:52,850 --> 00:06:55,610
OK, you can use the common keys.

So you start with a lowercase and then for every new word you use uppercase.

93

OK, with no space.

94

So you use this convention for reliable.

95

And this one for constants.

96

So you can know what is the difference between a valuable you can change and a constant.

97

Boulle is alive, OK, using those variables, those names for the violence will make much more sense

102

11
00:00:31,710 --> 00:00:32,940
So what do we have here?

12
00:00:32,940 --> 00:00:33,150
What?

13
00:00:33,150 --> 00:00:35,430
I have created two variables here.

14
00:00:35,430 --> 00:00:36,720
You can see number one.

15
00:00:36,720 --> 00:00:41,910
And number two, those variables are integer numbers three and five.

16

17
00:00:48,300 --> 00:00:51,800
number one, and then B, that is the double of number two.

18
00:00:51,810 --> 00:00:55,560
So let's say that what you want to do is simply to multiply the number by two.

19
00:00:55,560 --> 00:00:59,280
And so every time you're going to need to do the variable multiplied by two.

20
00:00:59,330 --> 00:01:05,190
Thing is this would be very inconvenient to always need to repeat the same code every time you need

21
00:01:05,190 --> 00:01:06,810

22
00:01:06,810 --> 00:01:11,670
And here it's a very small functionality, but the functionality can be much bigger with many lines

23
00:01:11,670 --> 00:01:12,240
of code.

24
00:01:12,660 --> 00:01:15,390
So what we need here is a function.

25
00:01:15,840 --> 00:01:22,530
A function is a reusable block of code that you can call from different parts of your program.

26
00:01:22,530 --> 00:01:24,840
And let's just create one to see what it is.

27
00:01:24,840 --> 00:01:26,730
So I'm just going to create a function here.

28
00:01:26,730 --> 00:01:31,290
Actually, void setup is already a function and void loop is a function.

29
00:01:31,740 --> 00:01:38,850
So let's create a new one here on top and let's call it double number like this, because we want to

30
00:01:38,850 --> 00:01:45,300
double a number and I'm going to make sure that I don't add any spaces for any new world.

31
00:01:45,300 --> 00:01:48,840
I just added new uppercase and you can see that at the beginning of the function.

32
00:01:48,840 --> 00:01:49,920
So that will be the name.

33
00:01:49,920 --> 00:01:54,780
But at the beginning of the function, we have something and we need to give the data type that we want

34

to return because the function will accept some parameters as an input and we'll return something as

35
00:02:01,590 --> 00:02:02,610
an output.

36
00:02:02,710 --> 00:02:06,150
And so here I'm just going to put INT and then space double number.

37
00:02:06,150 --> 00:02:10,230
That means that this function will return an integer number.

38
00:02:10,230 --> 00:02:16,260
So I put the data type the name, and then I open and close the parentheses and I have the definition

39
00:02:16,260 --> 00:02:17,070
of my function.

40
00:02:17,070 --> 00:02:19,720
You can see just like the void set up and the void loop.

41
00:02:19,740 --> 00:02:24,840
Now what I can also do is when I have a function, as I told you, we can give it an input.

42
00:02:24,840 --> 00:02:30,780
And so the input here, I'm just going to put int number, which means that the function can receive

43
00:02:30,780 --> 00:02:36,060
an integer value here that's going to be named number inside the function.

44
00:02:36,060 --> 00:02:41,610
And once I have this, I can just go back to a new line and open and close curly brackets.

45
00:02:41,820 --> 00:02:42,050
Okay.

46
00:02:42,060 --> 00:02:44,970
Very similar to what we did here with void, setup and void.

47
00:02:45,120 --> 00:02:48,900
But in this case you can see I have INT and I also have one parameter.

48
00:02:48,900 --> 00:02:51,600
You could have as many parameters as you want.

49
00:02:51,630 --> 00:02:54,180
You just separate them with a comma.

But let's just keep things simple for now with just one parameter.

51
00:02:58,200 --> 00:03:01,530
So this what is this function doing?

52
00:03:01,530 --> 00:03:06,210
Well, this function will need to actually multiply the number here by two.

53
00:03:06,300 --> 00:03:09,360
So let's just create a variable.

54
00:03:09,360 --> 00:03:15,030
INT result, for example, is equal to number multiplied by two.

55
00:03:15,840 --> 00:03:17,760
The number is the variable.

56
00:03:17,760 --> 00:03:21,750
As you can see that we get here in the parameter of the function.

57
00:03:21,750 --> 00:03:23,430
So that's what we do in the function.

58
00:03:23,430 --> 00:03:26,340
And then there is a keyword that you need to use in a function.

59
00:03:26,340 --> 00:03:31,350
It's called return can see turns green return result.

60
00:03:31,440 --> 00:03:33,240
So what do I return?

61
00:03:33,240 --> 00:03:37,800
I return this variable and this variable is an integer number.

62
00:03:37,800 --> 00:03:40,200
So that match is the type that I want to return.

63
00:03:40,440 --> 00:03:42,240
So now I have this block of code.

64
00:03:42,240 --> 00:03:48,390
You can see this block of code that's going to take as an input a number here, an integer number,

65
00:03:48,390 --> 00:03:51,150
and that's going to return another integer.

66
00:03:51,150 --> 00:03:57,960
But you can see this time the returned number is the double of the number that we pass as an input.

67
00:03:57,960 --> 00:04:01,410
And so once I have created a function, I can actually use it.

68
00:04:01,410 --> 00:04:08,280
And so here, instead of doing number one multiplied by two, I can just what we call calling the function.

69
00:04:08,280 --> 00:04:10,470
Actually, I can call the function.

70
00:04:10,470 --> 00:04:15,990
So double number and I pass number one.

71
00:04:16,920 --> 00:04:23,670
Double number, I call this function and I pass what I pass the number one variable here that I will

72
00:04:23,670 --> 00:04:26,280
pass to the input of the function.

73
00:04:26,280 --> 00:04:27,870
And so the number one will go here.

74
00:04:28,380 --> 00:04:34,560
It will be multiplied by two, and then we return the result, which means that it will go back here

75
00:04:34,560 --> 00:04:41,430
and then a will be equal to the result of this function, which is the double of the number.

76
00:04:41,730 --> 00:04:50,820
And I can do the same thing here, double number with number two, for example, I can give a different

77
00:04:50,820 --> 00:04:51,810
number every time.

78
00:04:51,810 --> 00:04:57,150
Okay, so that's a reusable block of code that I can use with a different number every time.

79
00:04:57,150 --> 00:04:57,600
Great.

80
00:04:57,600 --> 00:04:59,850
And that's a first example of a.

81
00:04:59,870 --> 00:05:03,680
Function and well, you can combine functions as much as you want.

82
00:05:03,680 --> 00:05:08,300
For example, you could call a function inside another function, and that's what we are going to do

83
00:05:08,300 --> 00:05:08,690
here.

84
00:05:08,690 --> 00:05:13,790
I'm going to create a function and I'm going to name it Void, and I'm going to explain to you void

85
00:05:13,790 --> 00:05:20,420
print double number and this one gets also int number.

86
00:05:20,660 --> 00:05:21,620
So what is this?

87
00:05:21,620 --> 00:05:24,890
Well, you can see that with a function you need to return data type.

88
00:05:24,890 --> 00:05:31,700
So you need to return, for example, an int or a float number or any other kind of data.

89
00:05:31,700 --> 00:05:36,260
But if you put void, void means that you will not return anything.

90
00:05:36,260 --> 00:05:39,530
And you can see that's actually what's happening in the void setup.

91
00:05:39,530 --> 00:05:45,560
This is a function and the void loop also is a function, but you don't have any return keyword because

92
00:05:45,560 --> 00:05:46,370
this is void.

93
00:05:46,790 --> 00:05:49,550
So we don't have any return keyword and that's what we do here.

94
00:05:49,550 --> 00:05:55,700
What we want to do is just to print the double of the number that we get from the parameter.

95
00:05:55,940 --> 00:06:00,710
So I put the void and then I put my opening and closing curly brackets.

96
00:06:00,710 --> 00:06:05,540
And what I can do here, let's say I want to so I want to print the double of this number.

97

But instead of doing number multiplied by two, I can just call this function so I could do serial dot

98

print ln to print something.

99

And what I can print is I can just do double number and I pass the number that I receive.

100
00:06:23,960 --> 00:06:28,100
I pass it here and I close all the parentheses.

101

So what's going to happen here is that if I call this function from here, let's say call it here,

102
00:06:35,180 --> 00:06:44,060
print double number with number one, what's going to happen is so we have created number one here and

103
00:06:44,060 --> 00:06:48,710
then we call the function named print double number with number one.

104
00:06:48,710 --> 00:06:49,910
So we go here.

105

The number one is going to be passed here.

So the int number in this function is going to become the value of number one.

107
00:06:58,220 --> 00:06:59,240
So it's going to be three.

108
00:06:59,750 --> 00:07:00,920
So here we have three.

109
00:07:01,430 --> 00:07:06,080
And then what we do is we do select println with double number.

110
00:07:06,080 --> 00:07:08,720
So here we are calling another function.

111
00:07:08,720 --> 00:07:12,110
So you see from one function we call another function.

112
00:07:12,350 --> 00:07:16,700
And so I call the double number and I pass what I get here as an input.

113
00:07:16,700 --> 00:07:20,060
I pass it here as an input for this function.

114
00:07:20,060 --> 00:07:23,330
So you can see I pass the values from function to function.

115
00:07:23,330 --> 00:07:29,000
And then this function is going to compute the double of the number, return the double of the number.

116
00:07:29,000 --> 00:07:30,430
So return the result.

117
00:07:30,440 --> 00:07:36,770
And so basically this here is going to be evaluated to the result of the function.

118
00:07:37,310 --> 00:07:39,470
So this will become the double of the number.

119
00:07:39,470 --> 00:07:42,050
And then what we do is we print the double of the number.

120
00:07:42,050 --> 00:07:42,680
All right?

121
00:07:42,770 --> 00:07:50,570
And so we can we can actually call it once I can call it twice with different numbers, etc., Actually,

122
00:07:50,570 --> 00:07:53,490
if we want to test, I would also need to initialize sales.

123
00:07:53,500 --> 00:07:55,190
So begin.

124
00:07:56,150 --> 00:07:57,980
Let's put this.

125
00:07:58,930 --> 00:07:59,770
Moderate.

126
00:08:00,840 --> 00:08:03,150
And let's actually run this.

127
00:08:04,990 --> 00:08:06,730
Lets name it functions.

128
00:08:07,790 --> 00:08:08,210
Okay.

129
00:08:08,330 --> 00:08:09,170
Don't uploading.

130
00:08:09,170 --> 00:08:14,780
If I open the sale monitor, you will see that we have six and ten.

131
00:08:15,260 --> 00:08:18,230
So six is the double of the number one.

132
00:08:18,230 --> 00:08:22,040
Three and ten is the double of the number two five here.

133
00:08:22,550 --> 00:08:24,680
So what I do, I just print double number.

134
00:08:24,680 --> 00:08:31,340
So I pass the three is going to go here to double number is going to go here is going to be return and

135
00:08:31,340 --> 00:08:32,120
we print it here.

136
00:08:32,480 --> 00:08:35,000
And then number two is going to be five.

137
00:08:35,000 --> 00:08:37,670
So we call this function with five.

138
00:08:37,910 --> 00:08:42,710
It goes here on line eight and then we call the one number on line two.

139
00:08:43,340 --> 00:08:49,460
So the number five is passed until here and then we reach on ten and then here we can print ten.

140
00:08:49,640 --> 00:08:56,540
And one thing we can maybe do also here as an improvement is that, well, in the double number function,

141
00:08:56,540 --> 00:09:00,980
what we do is we create a viable that we just return.

142
00:09:01,220 --> 00:09:05,880
So we don't use this variable for anything else than to return it.

143
00:09:05,900 --> 00:09:12,500
So instead of just creating that, I can just actually do return number multiplied by two.

144
00:09:13,730 --> 00:09:19,340
So if it's the last thing to do is you don't need to use the variable that you create, then you just

145
00:09:19,340 --> 00:09:22,580
can return whatever that you compute here directly.

146
00:09:22,580 --> 00:09:23,940
And this is going to work the same.

147
00:09:23,960 --> 00:09:24,350
All right.

148
00:09:24,350 --> 00:09:27,350
So that was a very quick introduction about functions.

149
00:09:27,350 --> 00:09:32,180
I know this is a quite complex topic when you begin, but don't be afraid if you don't understand everything,

150
00:09:32,180 --> 00:09:37,970
it will all make sense while we go through how the course with all of the different exercises.

151
00:09:37,970 --> 00:09:38,160
Okay.

152
00:09:38,210 --> 00:09:43,610
And later on, you can come back to this lesson and eventually it will make more sense.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,180 --> 00:00:00,500
Great.

2
00:00:00,540 --> 00:00:05,700
You now have a basic understanding of variables and functions all along the course.

3
00:00:05,700 --> 00:00:09,720
You will use them extensively and develop a better understanding with time.

4
00:00:09,720 --> 00:00:15,990
But there is one last important thing to understand about variables, and this is the scope of a variable.

5
00:00:16,350 --> 00:00:19,170
Depending on where you declare a variable.

6
00:00:19,170 --> 00:00:25,800
For example, if it's declared here or here, well, it may or may not be accessible from other parts

7
00:00:25,800 --> 00:00:26,880
of the program.

8
00:00:26,880 --> 00:00:29,100
And this is what we call the scope.

9
00:00:29,100 --> 00:00:35,550
So as you can see in this example, I create a variable named number one, and that's outside of any

10
00:00:35,550 --> 00:00:36,240
function.

11
00:00:36,420 --> 00:00:38,340
You can see this is outside of anything.

12
00:00:38,340 --> 00:00:41,970
And then I use number one here in this void set of function.

13
00:00:41,970 --> 00:00:48,450
But now you can see that I also create a variable A, but I create the variable A In the setup function,

14
00:00:48,450 --> 00:00:52,020
I create a variable A between those two curly brackets here.

15
00:00:52,620 --> 00:00:56,940
And let's see what will happen if I try to access this variable.

16
00:00:56,940 --> 00:00:58,230
A in the void loop.

17
00:00:58,230 --> 00:01:07,500
For example, if I do serial print ln with a like this, let's see what's going to happen if I try to

18
00:01:07,500 --> 00:01:08,280
compile.

19
00:01:09,220 --> 00:01:15,310
I'm going to have an error saying that A was not declared in the scope.

20
00:01:16,360 --> 00:01:18,610
What if I try to print number one?

21
00:01:19,120 --> 00:01:23,440
If I print number one, you see that it is going to work.

22
00:01:23,800 --> 00:01:29,950
But if I print a, I get this error not declared in the scope.

23
00:01:30,100 --> 00:01:31,290
So what is the scope?

24
00:01:31,300 --> 00:01:34,330
Basically with C++ and Arduino.

25
00:01:34,510 --> 00:01:37,810
The scope will just be something that's inside curly brackets.

26
00:01:38,020 --> 00:01:44,410
So when you create a new function, you will open and close curly brackets and later on with conditions

27
00:01:44,410 --> 00:01:45,160
and other stuff.

28
00:01:45,160 --> 00:01:48,000
You will also see that we open close curly brackets.

29
00:01:48,010 --> 00:01:55,540
And so when you have this here, it means that whatever is inside those curly brackets is inside a nested

30
00:01:55,540 --> 00:02:01,290
scope, a more nested scope than the global scope, which has no curly brackets by default.

31
00:02:01,300 --> 00:02:08,080
And so when you create a variable here in a more global scope and you use it in a more nested scope,

32
00:02:08,080 --> 00:02:09,220
then it's working.

33
00:02:09,430 --> 00:02:10,990
The variable is accessible.

34
00:02:10,990 --> 00:02:15,610
As you can see here, we have a more nested scope and we can access to number one.

35
00:02:15,610 --> 00:02:23,200
But here you can see I create the variable in this scope, so I can't use it outside of this scope.

36
00:02:23,680 --> 00:02:29,410
And you can see to go from the setup to the loop, we have to go out of the curly brackets and then

37
00:02:29,410 --> 00:02:31,600
we have to go inside another scope.

38
00:02:31,600 --> 00:02:38,230
So another set of curly brackets and that means that the variable A will not be accessible in this scope

39
00:02:38,230 --> 00:02:46,630
here, the variable A when it's created here, it will be accessible only in this scope and if you create

40
00:02:46,630 --> 00:02:47,230
another scope.

41
00:02:47,230 --> 00:02:52,150
So basically, if you have another set of curly brackets like this with conditions and stuff that we're

42
00:02:52,150 --> 00:02:56,650
going to see later, then you can access the variable here, no problem.

43
00:02:57,280 --> 00:03:01,720
But you cannot access it outside of the scope where it is created.

44
00:03:01,720 --> 00:03:09,160
So you cannot go out of the curly brackets and then expect that you can use this variable basically,

45
00:03:09,160 --> 00:03:10,030
that's the rule.

46
00:03:10,030 --> 00:03:16,420
So if you want to have a variable that you can use, for example in both setup and loop, you need to

47
00:03:16,420 --> 00:03:18,220
make it a global variable.

48
00:03:18,220 --> 00:03:23,080
So in the global scope like this, you can see it's not in any curly brackets.

49
00:03:23,080 --> 00:03:27,430
And as you could see with this example, the number one, we can use it actually in the setup and we

50
00:03:27,430 --> 00:03:28,810
can use it in the loop.

51
00:03:28,810 --> 00:03:34,510
But any variable that you create in the setup, you can only use it in the setup and not in the loop.

52
00:03:34,930 --> 00:03:40,450
And there is another thing is you can see that here we have so in this function and in that function

53
00:03:40,450 --> 00:03:46,120
we have a parameter for the function and this parameter for the function here, when you have this for

54
00:03:46,150 --> 00:03:51,730
function, this is also going to be a nested scope and we call that a local variable.

55
00:03:52,030 --> 00:03:58,030
We call that a local variable to the scope, which means that here the number, so the variable int

56
00:03:58,030 --> 00:04:02,380
number is going to be created here when you call the function.

57
00:04:02,380 --> 00:04:07,660
So the value that you pass to the function is going to create a new variable with this value.

58
00:04:07,660 --> 00:04:12,190
And this variable will only exist within the curly brackets here.

59
00:04:12,220 --> 00:04:17,500
After the curly brackets, the variable doesn't exist anymore, so you can't get access to it.

60
00:04:17,500 --> 00:04:22,360
And so that's why you can see that basically we have for example, int number here and we have int number

61
00:04:22,360 --> 00:04:22,810
here.

62
00:04:22,810 --> 00:04:25,750
We have the same name, but that's not a problem.

63
00:04:25,750 --> 00:04:30,340
It's not going to be a collision because of course you cannot create two variables with the same name.

64
00:04:30,340 --> 00:04:34,270
That's going to be a collision issue and you will have an error.

65
00:04:34,270 --> 00:04:40,180
But if the two variables are in the two different scopes, you can see here we have one scope and another

66
00:04:40,180 --> 00:04:42,910
scope because we have different set of curly brackets.

67
00:04:42,910 --> 00:04:47,290
So there is no collision, there is no problem between those scopes.

68
00:04:47,290 --> 00:04:49,480
You can create any variable you want here.

69
00:04:49,660 --> 00:04:53,500
You will not be able to access them in this scope.

70
00:04:53,500 --> 00:04:58,180
So basically what you can do is you can create the same name for the same variables, just like we did

71
00:04:58,180 --> 00:05:00,880
here into number and int number.

72
00:05:01,480 --> 00:05:03,610
And I want you to talk about the scope right now.

73
00:05:03,610 --> 00:05:09,340
I know it's a bit more complex, but I want you to talk about it now just so you can get a starting

74
00:05:09,340 --> 00:05:14,860
of an understanding because that's a very important concept and also because, well, you will probably

75
00:05:14,860 --> 00:05:20,290
have some errors when you start to program and some of those errors are going to be because of the scope.

76
00:05:20,290 --> 00:05:25,720
So when you have an error because of something like the scope, well, you can come back to this lesson

77
00:05:25,720 --> 00:05:27,670
and things will make more sense.

78
00:05:27,670 --> 00:05:31,510
You will understand more and maybe be able to fix your error.

79
00:05:31,540 --> 00:05:31,960
All right.

80
00:05:31,960 --> 00:05:37,570
So to recap on the scope, when you create a variable, pay attention to the scope.

81
00:05:37,720 --> 00:05:45,520
A variable is only accessible in its own scope or in any nested scope inside the scope.

82
00:05:46,030 --> 00:05:50,710
Thus, you could have different variables with the same name in different scopes.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,180 --> 00:00:06,210
So far, you have seen that all the instructions you write in and out a program will be executed one

2
00:00:06,210 --> 00:00:13,140
by one, the key line to be executed and then laid three and I'm falling five, etc. But as you progress,

3
00:00:13,140 --> 00:00:19,650
you will want to make your program more dynamic and you will want to be able to execute only some instructions,

4
00:00:19,930 --> 00:00:22,990
depending on some conditions that you test.

5
00:00:23,390 --> 00:00:30,690
For example, you might do a certain action if a push button is priced and if it's not pressed, you

6
00:00:30,690 --> 00:00:32,010
would do another action.

7
00:00:32,220 --> 00:00:35,970
So let's write our first condition to understand how it works.

8
00:00:36,220 --> 00:00:40,410
So let's create a viable in the voice set, let's say temperature.

9
00:00:40,410 --> 00:00:45,280
We will use integer, OK, to make it simpler and let's see 30 degrees.

10
00:00:45,660 --> 00:00:52,350
So as you can see, this variable is declared inside the setup function, which means that the label

11
00:00:52,350 --> 00:00:57,990
is local to the set of function and we won't be able to use it like that in the loop function because

12
00:00:57,990 --> 00:00:59,170
this is a different scope.

13
00:00:59,490 --> 00:00:59,880
Don't know.

14
00:00:59,880 --> 00:01:04,640
Let's say we want to check if the temperature is equal to some value.

15
00:01:04,920 --> 00:01:12,360
Well, to create a condition, you can just use if the if you want or it should green then space and

16
00:01:12,360 --> 00:01:17,510
then you will open parenthesis and introduces, you will make a test.

17
00:01:17,980 --> 00:01:19,800
I'm just going to write one.

18
00:01:19,980 --> 00:01:24,830
Temperature is equal to, let's say, 20 degrees.

19
00:01:25,350 --> 00:01:25,730
All right.

20
00:01:25,740 --> 00:01:30,730
And you can see to test the equality we need to put to equal sign.

21
00:01:30,780 --> 00:01:34,710
OK, one equal sign is to assign a value.

22
00:01:34,710 --> 00:01:42,180
So to assign the value from the right to the step on the left to equal sine will simply test if the

23
00:01:42,180 --> 00:01:44,670
value on the left is equal to the value on the right.

24
00:01:44,940 --> 00:01:51,810
And if it's this, we return a boolean, which value is true, and if not, it will return boolean,

25
00:01:51,810 --> 00:01:53,520
which value is false.

26
00:01:54,010 --> 00:02:00,960
So now you have written your IFPRI the condition in parentheses you can go back to a new line and open

27
00:02:00,960 --> 00:02:09,450
close brackets so you can either open the brackets here or here on the same line as if this is just

28
00:02:09,450 --> 00:02:16,230
a matter of preference and readability, though not if the temperature is exactly equal to 20.

29
00:02:16,410 --> 00:02:18,890
And we are going to do some action here.

30
00:02:19,020 --> 00:02:23,720
OK, if not, then the block of code here will not be executed.

31
00:02:24,570 --> 00:02:26,250
Let's say say OK, print Ellen.

32
00:02:27,360 --> 00:02:31,110
Let's put simply, OK, let's run that program.

33
00:02:31,320 --> 00:02:35,420
So first we need of course, to to been.

34
00:02:41,760 --> 00:02:42,510
Conditions.

35
00:02:45,980 --> 00:02:52,520
And as you will see, well, nothing gets executed here if we use 30.

36
00:02:55,420 --> 00:03:04,270
We will see, OK, because the conditions here will be evaluated to true, OK, so this is great because

37
00:03:04,270 --> 00:03:10,410
it means you can decide to execute this block of code only if the condition here is true.

38
00:03:10,750 --> 00:03:12,820
So you have different operators.

39
00:03:12,820 --> 00:03:19,800
You have the equal equal which will test the equality, then you might want to test the inequality.

40
00:03:20,170 --> 00:03:24,080
In this case, you will use exclamation mark and equal.

41
00:03:24,700 --> 00:03:32,040
So if the temperature is different than 30 degrees of any value but here, which is different than 30,

42
00:03:32,350 --> 00:03:33,660
this condition would be true.

43
00:03:33,670 --> 00:03:35,740
And we enter this book of code.

44
00:03:36,010 --> 00:03:42,750
Now, you can also test with the lower than this mean strictly lower then.

45
00:03:43,510 --> 00:03:49,660
So if the temperature here is equal to 29 or lower, then this will work.

46
00:03:49,870 --> 00:03:55,210
If it's equal to 30, it will not work because this means strictly lower.

47
00:03:55,210 --> 00:03:58,270
Then then you have the lower are equal.

48
00:03:58,300 --> 00:04:01,990
So this is the bracket plus equals sign.

49
00:04:02,320 --> 00:04:11,440
And if the temperature is 30, that will also what you can test also with greater than the temperature

50
00:04:11,440 --> 00:04:13,430
is strictly greater than 30.

51
00:04:13,480 --> 00:04:20,180
So starting at 31, you can test with the greater are equal than 30.

52
00:04:20,560 --> 00:04:23,680
Now this will test just one condition.

53
00:04:23,830 --> 00:04:29,190
You might want to test different conditions and combine them together.

54
00:04:29,500 --> 00:04:36,490
For example, if you want to check if the temperature is between 20 and 29, what you could do is check

55
00:04:36,490 --> 00:04:42,610
first if the temperature is greater or equal than 20.

56
00:04:43,830 --> 00:04:54,210
OK, and then you can use what we call end Kastel to ampersands, this will combine two conditions,

57
00:04:54,690 --> 00:05:01,960
but you need to put this into parenthesis here and then put another condition to Currentzis and let's

58
00:05:01,960 --> 00:05:03,270
say temperature.

59
00:05:04,310 --> 00:05:06,260
Is lower than.

60
00:05:07,660 --> 00:05:12,140
Urges a strictly lower and well what this will do.

61
00:05:12,160 --> 00:05:19,180
So you entered the chief and then you see that you have and between two conditions that this condition

62
00:05:19,180 --> 00:05:20,190
will be evaluated.

63
00:05:20,620 --> 00:05:26,650
But if the temperature is greater or equal than 20 and then this one will be evaluated and still the

64
00:05:26,650 --> 00:05:33,460
temperature is strictly lower than 30 and then and will basically return true for the whole condition.

65
00:05:33,820 --> 00:05:41,340
If both conditions are true, if I put 25, this will work because this is true.

66
00:05:42,210 --> 00:05:43,600
OK, this is true.

67
00:05:43,870 --> 00:05:46,810
And thus the whole stuff is true.

68
00:05:46,810 --> 00:05:48,390
And then we enter this book of code.

69
00:05:48,910 --> 00:05:51,490
So we are going to see that also later.

70
00:05:51,940 --> 00:05:59,640
So you have the end operator, which will be true only if everything is true and you have the operator,

71
00:05:59,950 --> 00:06:01,660
but you can use two pipes.

72
00:06:02,440 --> 00:06:09,880
And with this operator, the whole condition will be true if at least one of the conditions is true.

73
00:06:10,630 --> 00:06:15,040
So if this condition is true, let's say but 35 now.

74
00:06:15,520 --> 00:06:17,420
So the temperature is greater than 20.

75
00:06:17,450 --> 00:06:21,160
OK, this is true that this condition will be false.

76
00:06:22,360 --> 00:06:28,320
But then with the operator, because this condition is true, then this one can be true or false.

77
00:06:28,330 --> 00:06:29,110
It doesn't matter.

78
00:06:29,350 --> 00:06:33,050
The whole condition will be true and is a book of good will be executed.

79
00:06:33,430 --> 00:06:33,840
All right.

80
00:06:33,850 --> 00:06:35,230
And that's not all.

81
00:06:35,230 --> 00:06:36,730
We have the conditions.

82
00:06:37,390 --> 00:06:43,240
We have a simple if in any condition here, you are going to test something and execute this block of

83
00:06:43,240 --> 00:06:50,000
code, but maybe you can also decide to execute something if the condition is not true.

84
00:06:50,020 --> 00:06:58,480
OK, so if this is false, then you can use the false statements just after the if block here.

85

You create a new block of code and this will be executed in the condition here is false.

Let's just put a simple condition.

Temperature.

Is equal to Secretary Kerry Prince, OK, and if.

It's not funny we print not poking, it's upload in code.

Let's see what happens.

Not OK, but what happened here?

We entered.

If we check the condition, the condition is false.
