00:07:39,850 --> 00:07:43,980
And what you can also do is add intermediate blocks else.

98
00:07:44,080 --> 00:07:46,570
If so, what you can do.

99
00:07:47,740 --> 00:07:50,170
Is to test different conditions.

100
00:07:53,770 --> 00:08:00,160
So let's say we want to first test if the temperature is 40, then we print these and then the hold

101
00:08:00,700 --> 00:08:03,970
if as if a structure is over.

102
00:08:04,000 --> 00:08:06,900
So after this, we just go to line 50.

103
00:08:07,240 --> 00:08:12,540
But if this condition is not true, then we are going to test the next one.

104
00:08:12,760 --> 00:08:15,550
If this condition is true, we enter this block of code.

105
00:08:19,090 --> 00:08:21,670
And then it's as if.

106
00:08:23,770 --> 00:08:25,060
And let's see what happens.

107
00:08:28,120 --> 00:08:33,210
OK, we see it because this condition is false, which is the signal on this condition is true.

108
00:08:33,490 --> 00:08:37,300
And then we prayed that if I use another value.

109
00:08:40,700 --> 00:08:45,920
You can see we test for the condition here and then the condition here and then because none of the

110
00:08:45,920 --> 00:08:50,750
condition is true, we don't under any block of code and we enter the else block of code.

111
00:08:50,930 --> 00:08:51,350
All right.

112
00:08:51,350 --> 00:08:58,160
And one more thing to know is also, again, to pay attention to this code here, the number two available

113
00:08:58,160 --> 00:09:00,800
with created in the scope of this set to function.

114
00:09:00,830 --> 00:09:05,120
OK, you can see here we have curly brackets, which means this is a new school.

115
00:09:05,270 --> 00:09:06,890
This is a nested scope.

116
00:09:07,190 --> 00:09:12,950
So we can use the term approach available inside the scope if you want, because it is nested compared

117
00:09:12,950 --> 00:09:15,920
to the scope where the temperature was created.

118
00:09:16,790 --> 00:09:24,530
So to recap and conditions, you will use a structure when you want to do a different action depending

119
00:09:24,530 --> 00:09:27,620
on some conditions that you want to test.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,110 --> 00:00:02,690
Almost done with the programming basics.

2
00:00:02,720 --> 00:00:06,200
Now let's look at loops.

3
00:00:06,200 --> 00:00:08,750
And again let's first understand why.

4
00:00:08,840 --> 00:00:12,590
Here is a basic example that I'm going to run.

5
00:00:13,560 --> 00:00:14,880
So loops.

6
00:00:15,510 --> 00:00:18,360
And as you can see, this is quite stupid.

7
00:00:18,360 --> 00:00:24,150
But we want to print hello ten times and you can see hello ten times.

8
00:00:24,150 --> 00:00:28,440
And here it's stupid because we just need to write println.

9
00:00:28,440 --> 00:00:29,910
Hello ten times.

10
00:00:29,910 --> 00:00:33,270
What happens if I need to print hello a thousand times?

11
00:00:33,270 --> 00:00:39,930
Well, whenever you need to repeat something more than two times, this would be best to use a loop.

12
00:00:39,930 --> 00:00:42,990
So here I'm going to show you two kinds of loop.

13
00:00:42,990 --> 00:00:45,330
The for loop and the while loop.

14
00:00:45,330 --> 00:00:48,240
Let's first see how to use the for loop.

15
00:00:48,240 --> 00:00:51,870
So I'm going to just remove all of that.

16
00:00:52,200 --> 00:00:53,910
And let's use a for loop.

17
00:00:54,180 --> 00:00:56,820
So you can start with four.

18
00:00:56,850 --> 00:00:58,950
You can see the keyword for in green.

19
00:00:58,950 --> 00:01:01,080
Then you open the parenthesis okay.

20
00:01:01,080 --> 00:01:04,110
And then I'm going to write it and explain it just after.

21
00:01:04,110 --> 00:01:07,650
So int I is equal to zero.

22
00:01:08,760 --> 00:01:12,030
And then semicolon I lower than ten.

23
00:01:12,030 --> 00:01:20,220
Semicolon I plus plus close the parentheses I open curly brackets, I press enter.

24
00:01:20,220 --> 00:01:26,910
This automatically closes the curly brackets block and adds a two space indentation.

25
00:01:26,910 --> 00:01:30,900
And then I can just put this right here.

26
00:01:32,770 --> 00:01:33,100
Right.

27
00:01:33,100 --> 00:01:35,410
And this let's let's run this.

28
00:01:35,410 --> 00:01:40,180
This will do the exact same thing, but with just three lines of code.

29
00:01:40,540 --> 00:01:43,120
Okay, you can see hello ten times.

30
00:01:43,420 --> 00:01:47,500
So here a little bit more because we already had I'm going to clear the output.

31
00:01:50,500 --> 00:01:51,430
Okay ten times.

32
00:01:51,430 --> 00:01:55,210
We have already had data from the previous run on the serial monitor.

33
00:01:55,510 --> 00:01:55,930
So.

34
00:01:55,930 --> 00:01:57,400
Well, how to create a follow?

35
00:01:57,430 --> 00:01:58,900
This is quite simple.

36
00:01:58,900 --> 00:02:05,200
You have three different things you need to give in the parenthesis, separated by semicolons.

37
00:02:05,230 --> 00:02:07,330
First is where you start.

38
00:02:07,330 --> 00:02:09,040
So here I create.

39
00:02:09,040 --> 00:02:13,300
You can see directly inside the int I create a variable.

40
00:02:13,300 --> 00:02:15,400
So I declare it and initialize it.

41
00:02:15,400 --> 00:02:20,980
So this will be the index we are going to use for the loop.

42
00:02:20,980 --> 00:02:24,040
And I initialize it here to zero okay.

43
00:02:24,040 --> 00:02:25,930
So that is the starting point.

44
00:02:26,050 --> 00:02:33,040
And then the second stuff in the for loop is the condition for the loop to continue.

45
00:02:33,040 --> 00:02:37,240
So here the condition is I is strictly lower than ten.

46
00:02:37,240 --> 00:02:44,230
So basically as long as I is strictly lower than ten we are going to execute this block of code.

47
00:02:44,230 --> 00:02:51,880
And the third parameter here is what we do with the variable with the index every time.

48
00:02:51,880 --> 00:02:55,540
And here I do I plus plus I plus plus.

49
00:02:55,540 --> 00:03:02,170
So I plus plus is the same as if you do I is equal to I plus one.

50
00:03:02,680 --> 00:03:05,920
This will simply add one to the variable.

51
00:03:06,490 --> 00:03:08,560
And this is specific to c and C plus plus.

52
00:03:08,560 --> 00:03:10,030
And it's quite convenient.

53
00:03:10,030 --> 00:03:13,030
So what we do we start at zero.

54
00:03:13,030 --> 00:03:15,970
We check if zero is strictly lower than ten.

55
00:03:15,970 --> 00:03:20,140
Yes we enter the four block and then we come back to this.

56
00:03:20,290 --> 00:03:23,590
So what we do is we do I plus plus.

57
00:03:23,590 --> 00:03:25,840
So now I is equal to one.

58
00:03:25,840 --> 00:03:27,820
Is one strictly lower than ten.

59
00:03:27,820 --> 00:03:28,300
Yes.

60
00:03:28,300 --> 00:03:33,910
So we enter this block etc. etc. until we reach value ten.

61
00:03:33,910 --> 00:03:38,680
So when I is equal to ten this condition will not be true okay.

62
00:03:38,680 --> 00:03:42,130
Because ten is not strictly lower than ten.

63
00:03:42,130 --> 00:03:47,230
And then we don't enter the block and we resume the program here on line seven.

64
00:03:47,230 --> 00:03:53,110
And as you can see here, a particularity is that we start to count at zero.

65
00:03:53,110 --> 00:03:53,440
Okay.

66
00:03:53,440 --> 00:03:55,360
In programming, take this habit.

67
00:03:55,360 --> 00:04:01,990
You don't start to count at one like you would do in real life, but you start to count at zero.

68
00:04:01,990 --> 00:04:06,460
So to count from 1 to 10, you will actually count from 0 to 9.

69
00:04:06,460 --> 00:04:06,940
Great.

70
00:04:06,940 --> 00:04:11,980
And we are going to come back to this for loop quite a few times in this course because this is very,

71
00:04:11,980 --> 00:04:12,910
very useful.

72
00:04:12,910 --> 00:04:16,630
And now another loop you can use is while.

73
00:04:16,930 --> 00:04:23,680
So I'm going to do the exact same thing we did here, which is to print that ten times but using a different

74
00:04:23,680 --> 00:04:25,030
way with the while loop.

75
00:04:25,510 --> 00:04:30,310
So first I'm going to create an int I.

76
00:04:30,940 --> 00:04:32,980
So I'm creating an index okay.

77
00:04:33,310 --> 00:04:36,400
And then the while basically while condition.

78
00:04:36,400 --> 00:04:43,330
So while I is lower strictly than ten I'm going to open curly brackets.

79
00:04:43,630 --> 00:04:46,450
And I'm going to do this.

80
00:04:46,840 --> 00:04:49,510
So while I is strictly lower than ten I print.

81
00:04:49,510 --> 00:04:50,260
Hello.

82
00:04:50,260 --> 00:04:55,390
So here let's say hello four and hello while so we can make the difference.

83
00:04:55,750 --> 00:04:59,650
And what I need to do is I plus plus okay.

84
00:04:59,650 --> 00:05:02,710
And don't forget the semicolon after each statement.

85
00:05:04,120 --> 00:05:06,700
Let's run that code here.

86
00:05:06,700 --> 00:05:10,390
So I'm going to clear the output and upload.

87
00:05:13,520 --> 00:05:17,750
Okay, you can see hello for ten times and hello while ten times.

88
00:05:18,080 --> 00:05:23,270
So you can see the while is a little bit different because we just test one condition and then we go

89
00:05:23,270 --> 00:05:24,170
inside the block.

90
00:05:24,170 --> 00:05:31,760
So if you want to use a counter like we used in the for, you would need to create a counter here before

91
00:05:31,760 --> 00:05:32,360
the while.

92
00:05:32,360 --> 00:05:37,850
And then you of course need to increase the counter inside the while because if you don't increase it

93
00:05:37,850 --> 00:05:40,820
well, I will always be lower than ten.

94
00:05:40,820 --> 00:05:41,060
Okay.

95
00:05:41,060 --> 00:05:42,110
If you don't increase I.

96
00:05:42,140 --> 00:05:44,900
So this condition will just run indefinitely.

97
00:05:44,900 --> 00:05:49,160
And this is what we call an infinite loop.

98
00:05:49,430 --> 00:05:54,560
So an infinite loop is when you are stuck in one loop and you can't get outside of the loop.

99
00:05:54,560 --> 00:06:01,430
So here, because we always increase by one, at some point I will be equal to ten and this condition

100
00:06:01,430 --> 00:06:02,210
will be false.

101
00:06:02,210 --> 00:06:05,060
So we continue.

102
00:06:05,060 --> 00:06:08,270
So we resume the program at line 30 okay.

103
00:06:08,270 --> 00:06:10,160
And here in the while we use a counter.

104
00:06:10,160 --> 00:06:12,230
But you can actually use something different.

105
00:06:12,230 --> 00:06:16,730
For example while while the push button is pressed do this.

106
00:06:16,730 --> 00:06:21,380
And then once the push button is not pressed anymore we just leave the while.

107
00:06:21,410 --> 00:06:21,920
Great.

108
00:06:21,920 --> 00:06:25,490
So now when to use a for loop versus a while loop.

109
00:06:25,490 --> 00:06:31,310
Basically, if you know how many times you need to go through the loop, use a for loop.

110
00:06:31,370 --> 00:06:38,930
For example, if you need 100 samples from a sensor, then go with a for loop and use a while loop when

111
00:06:38,930 --> 00:06:42,080
you don't necessarily know when you have to stop.

112
00:06:42,560 --> 00:06:49,700
For example, if you need to read data from the same sensor but stop whenever the value reaches a certain

113
00:06:49,700 --> 00:06:50,660
threshold.

114
00:06:50,660 --> 00:06:57,890
So for the example of this lesson, we can clearly see that a for loop was the most appropriate, since

115
00:06:57,890 --> 00:07:01,610
we knew exactly how many times we had to repeat the action.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,300 --> 00:00:06,840
And to complete this programming section, let's have a look at the race you have seen that you can

2
00:00:06,840 --> 00:00:08,640
store data into valuables.

3
00:00:09,060 --> 00:00:14,290
Now, what if you need to store 10 values, for example, 10 symbols you got from a census?

4
00:00:14,880 --> 00:00:17,430
And what if you need to store 8000 votes?

5
00:00:17,790 --> 00:00:23,450
You can guess that declaring thousand valuables in your code is not practical at all.

6
00:00:23,870 --> 00:00:27,900
So this is where you will use arrays are what we can also call lists.

7
00:00:28,290 --> 00:00:29,510
What is an array?

8
00:00:29,730 --> 00:00:36,280
An array is simply a collection of values that are related and share the same data type.

9
00:00:36,840 --> 00:00:43,200
For example, you could have an array to store all students name from a classroom, another array to

10
00:00:43,200 --> 00:00:47,310
store the values you read from a photoresistor, etc..

11
00:00:47,670 --> 00:00:51,420
So let's create an array here in the global scope.

12
00:00:51,990 --> 00:00:57,090
For example, we want to store list of temperatures as integers to make it simple.

13
00:00:57,390 --> 00:01:03,270
But first you need the data type of the values you are going to store in the array and then you are

14
00:01:03,270 --> 00:01:09,840
going to create a name for the array, just like you would create a name for variable temperature.

15
00:01:10,350 --> 00:01:15,960
So one common practice, if you want to start the list of temperature, then you can simply go to a

16
00:01:15,990 --> 00:01:18,820
temperature list or temperature.

17
00:01:18,990 --> 00:01:19,390
All right.

18
00:01:19,440 --> 00:01:24,600
OK, this is basically the same thing and this will make sense for you in the future.

19
00:01:24,990 --> 00:01:32,940
Then you open close brackets and inside brackets, you will need to define the size of the OK.

20
00:01:32,940 --> 00:01:41,010
Once you declare an array in C and C++ for Arduino, you first need to put a size and this size can't

21
00:01:41,010 --> 00:01:42,390
be modified after what?

22
00:01:42,720 --> 00:01:50,400
Let's say I want five elements in my array and here I have declared a three with five integer elements,

23
00:01:50,730 --> 00:01:53,960
but I have not initialized the value yet.

24
00:01:54,450 --> 00:01:55,470
Let's compile.

25
00:01:56,600 --> 00:01:57,410
The Rai's.

26
00:02:00,010 --> 00:02:06,670
OK, and now what I can do is I can initialize so I can initialize later on in the code or directly

27
00:02:06,670 --> 00:02:07,240
here.

28
00:02:07,930 --> 00:02:12,210
So one option I have is simply to put zero into brackets.

29
00:02:12,610 --> 00:02:19,900
This will initialize the array so all of the elements of the array to zero and then I can modify them

30
00:02:20,530 --> 00:02:21,040
if I want.

31
00:02:21,040 --> 00:02:24,160
I can also directly set a value for each element.

32
00:02:24,520 --> 00:02:25,750
So we have five elements.

33
00:02:25,750 --> 00:02:32,310
Let's say twenty three, twenty four, fifty six, 12 and 14.

34
00:02:33,670 --> 00:02:43,030
And this will put all of those values in all of the indexes of the array and know how to walk with this

35
00:02:43,030 --> 00:02:43,330
array.

36
00:02:43,330 --> 00:02:46,610
So here you can initialize it to work with the array.

37
00:02:46,660 --> 00:02:49,160
Well I'm going to do Sanyal.

38
00:02:50,690 --> 00:02:51,600
But being.

39
00:02:54,920 --> 00:03:04,190
And let's say we want to print the print, Ellen, we want to print the value 24 fonderie to get access

40
00:03:04,430 --> 00:03:06,530
to an element of the array.

41
00:03:06,560 --> 00:03:12,980
You first need to write the name of the right temperature, the right, and then you put some brackets

42
00:03:12,980 --> 00:03:16,910
and you write the index of the element you want.

43
00:03:17,060 --> 00:03:21,090
And in programming, we start to count at zero.

44
00:03:21,170 --> 00:03:23,690
If you'll remember, we don't start to count at one.

45
00:03:24,080 --> 00:03:31,820
So here you have five numbers, but in the index for this number is zero one, two, three and four.

46
00:03:32,300 --> 00:03:40,640
So if I want to have the value here, I need the index one and let's load and check in the same monitor.

47
00:03:43,110 --> 00:03:49,230
Yes, I have the volume 24, which is the second element of the array, which is the index number one.

48
00:03:49,800 --> 00:03:56,420
So now pay attention to that when you use the index to make sure you don't go after the last you here,

49
00:03:56,430 --> 00:03:59,690
the last index will be basically the size minus one.

50
00:04:00,060 --> 00:04:03,360
So don't try to go over the floor then.

51
00:04:03,360 --> 00:04:11,310
If you want to modify a value inside the array, whether you simply to the temperature array, you put

52
00:04:11,760 --> 00:04:14,940
the index to, let's say I want to modify the first one.

53
00:04:15,970 --> 00:04:23,860
So actually, index number one would be the second element, and let's put it this will put this value

54
00:04:23,860 --> 00:04:28,810
inside the element of the rate which corresponds to index one.

55
00:04:29,650 --> 00:04:35,500
If I print again after that, you would see that the value has changed.

56
00:04:35,980 --> 00:04:39,560
It was so twenty four and then it is thirty.

57
00:04:40,480 --> 00:04:46,570
OK, so you can see how to get access to one element, how to modify one element.

58
00:04:46,840 --> 00:04:53,110
And then if you want to go through all of the elements of the array, well you can use the for loop

59
00:04:53,110 --> 00:04:54,760
that we have just discovered before.

60
00:04:55,450 --> 00:04:59,820
So for int I is equal to zero.

61
00:05:00,340 --> 00:05:07,540
So we are going to start with an index at zero and then I is lower than the size of the area.

62
00:05:07,570 --> 00:05:10,540
So five so here is strictly lower.

63
00:05:10,570 --> 00:05:10,760
OK.

64
00:05:10,780 --> 00:05:16,630
This is very important because you don't want to get to the value five because five will be outside

65
00:05:16,630 --> 00:05:24,340
of the array because we start to count at zero and then I guess plus you close the parentheses, you

66
00:05:24,340 --> 00:05:27,790
open a new block of code and let's say serial that.

67
00:05:28,930 --> 00:05:34,450
Brent L.N., so what you can do is you can do temperature.

68
00:05:36,230 --> 00:05:44,920
All right, open the brackets and then the index here will be high so you can use actually the counter

69
00:05:45,350 --> 00:05:51,410
index we use in the fall, you can use it in the block of code because this is a valuable you create.

So this is a local valuable.

71
00:05:52,850 --> 00:05:58,250
You create all the block of code that followed so you can use the eye.

72
00:05:58,430 --> 00:06:03,620
And I would be equal to zero one, two, three and four and then exit.

73
00:06:03,830 --> 00:06:05,180
So don't forget this.

74
00:06:06,020 --> 00:06:11,510
I'm going to comment those two lines here so we can print out of death.

75
00:06:12,140 --> 00:06:15,710
Let's upload the program and let's see what we get.

76
00:06:17,880 --> 00:06:24,390
OK, you can see 23, which is the first one, 30, which is the second value that was modified here,

77
00:06:24,810 --> 00:06:26,750
and then fifty six, 12 and 14.

78
00:06:26,770 --> 00:06:31,040
So we have just gone through all of the elements in the area.

79
00:06:31,350 --> 00:06:37,620
And these we are going to use the exact same structure later on when we use multiple entities in our

80
00:06:37,620 --> 00:06:38,060
program.

81
00:06:38,280 --> 00:06:39,960
So that would be malpractice for you.

82
00:06:40,260 --> 00:06:48,600
OK, to rig up an array is a collection of related valuables sharing the same data to create an array,

83
00:06:48,600 --> 00:06:53,150
you first need to declare it and give it a size which cannot be changed afterwards.

84
00:06:53,580 --> 00:06:58,430
Then you can access and modify any value from using an index.

85
00:06:58,860 --> 00:07:05,400
Finally, using full loop is a pretty efficient way of going through all the values from an array.

00:00:00,180 --> 00:00:07,040
Let's come back to our digital pings on the audio, as you can see, some of the pings have a bit of

2

volume function.

00:00:07,980 --> 00:00:15,030

4
00:00:15,030 --> 00:00:18,000
Need a number for the pin on Arduino.

5
00:00:18,060 --> 00:00:25,120
You know, out of the 14 digital pings, six of them can be used as output beams with a bit of value

6
00:00:25,120 --> 00:00:32,580
when they are pings, number three, five, six, nine, 10 and 11.

7
00:00:33,420 --> 00:00:40,810
Now, what is a bit of a win win means both with modulation.

8
00:00:41,400 --> 00:00:43,290
That's quite a complicated name.

9
00:00:43,290 --> 00:00:49,560
But you will see it's quite easy to understand when you use a digital pin with a binary state.

10
00:00:49,860 --> 00:00:57,240
Either the voltage is high, which means five all the time are low, which means zero volt all the time.

11
00:00:57,840 --> 00:01:06,030
With a pinger win, you can choose to make the signal high only a fraction of the time, and this fraction

12
00:01:06,030 --> 00:01:10,410
of the time in percentage is called the duty cycle.

13
00:01:10,680 --> 00:01:13,020
So here we have three examples.

14
00:01:13,290 --> 00:01:16,240
First, we have a duty cycle of zero percent.

15
00:01:16,680 --> 00:01:21,020
This means that the signal will be high zero percent of the time.

16
00:01:21,540 --> 00:01:23,950
Thus the signal is always low.

17
00:01:24,420 --> 00:01:29,970
In practice, this is the exact same as just using digital right with low.

18
00:01:30,720 --> 00:01:36,890
If we look at the third example on the bottom here, we have a duty cycle of 100 percent.

19
00:01:37,410 --> 00:01:41,990
That means that signal will be high 100 percent of the time.

20
00:01:42,570 --> 00:01:45,050
Thus the signal is always higher.

21
00:01:45,510 --> 00:01:50,320
In practice, this is the exact same as just using digital rights with height.

22
00:01:50,880 --> 00:01:56,700
And now the interesting part, if you look at the example in the middle, you can see we have a duty

23
00:01:56,700 --> 00:01:58,990
cycle of 25 percent.

24
00:01:59,490 --> 00:02:08,040
This means that the signal will be high 25 percent of the time and low 75 percent of the time.

25
00:02:08,610 --> 00:02:15,000
In practice, for example, when you go on and instead using the P the volume of a digital.

26
00:02:15,720 --> 00:02:22,740
Well, if you see 25 percent of the duty cycle, it's very similar as if instead of supplying five votes

27
00:02:22,740 --> 00:02:31,230
to the pin, you just supplied one point twenty five volt, which corresponds to 25 percent of the vote.

28
00:02:31,680 --> 00:02:37,710
Thus, you can expect the energy to be both on that with a lower intensity.

29
00:02:38,280 --> 00:02:45,210
To make it simple, just think about the duty cycle of the people, um, as a percentage of the total

30
00:02:45,360 --> 00:02:46,070
voltage.

31
00:02:46,470 --> 00:02:53,040
And you can see that if you modify the volume duty cycle between zero and one hundred percent, you

32
00:02:53,040 --> 00:02:58,110
can have much more control over the average voltages supply to your components.

33
00:02:58,500 --> 00:03:07,080
Now on the Arduino, the percentage will be represented by an integer number between zero and two hundred

34
00:03:07,080 --> 00:03:13,860
fifty five zero is, of course, zero percent and two hundred fifty five is one hundred percent.

35
00:03:14,100 --> 00:03:20,550
If you want to apply a 25 percent duty cycle for the PITIABLY, when you will then need to give twenty

36
00:03:20,550 --> 00:03:25,250
five percent of 255, which is about 64.

37
00:03:25,830 --> 00:03:27,820
So let's complete our diagram.

38
00:03:28,320 --> 00:03:33,630
We are still setting the mode for the digital Pinas output and then we have two options.

39
00:03:34,020 --> 00:03:40,140
Either we use the PIN with a binary state, which means we use the digital right function with a high

40
00:03:40,390 --> 00:03:41,810
or low ball.

And this only applies to things with the PWI in March, we can set an analog value using the analog

42

right function, which we are going to see in the next listen and provide a value between zero and 255,

43
00:03:56,880 --> 00:04:00,360
which corresponds to the percentage of the duty cycle.

7

9
00:00:37,950 --> 00:00:44,570
And when you choose input, then what you can do is to read a digital value from the component.

10
00:00:45,030 --> 00:00:49,230
This value will be binary, either high or low.

11
00:00:49,650 --> 00:00:56,640
If the voltage on the pin is close to five five-fold, you will get the value high and if the voltage

12
00:00:56,640 --> 00:01:00,510
is close to zero volt, you will get the value low.

13
00:01:00,810 --> 00:01:06,980
Note that you can't read analog values when using a digital pen as an input pen.

14

15

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,330 --> 00:00:02,620
Let's understand how things work.

2
00:00:02,940 --> 00:00:10,170
So if you remember, we have 600 pins on the argument, the number for those pings goes from a zero

3
00:00:10,170 --> 00:00:11,070
to a five.

4
00:00:11,730 --> 00:00:17,940
Note that on other types of around the world, you may also have a different layout, a different number

5
00:00:17,940 --> 00:00:22,810
of Europeans that if you understand this lesson well, it would be easy for you to find it.

6
00:00:23,340 --> 00:00:32,010
And one thing you can notice here is that is not just an analog underpins its written analog in which

7
00:00:32,010 --> 00:00:33,730
means analog input.

8
00:00:34,170 --> 00:00:42,180
So by default, the analog pings on the original are set as input beings, which means that in your

9
00:00:42,180 --> 00:00:44,970
code you would not have to call the pinboard function first.

10
00:00:45,420 --> 00:00:49,530
You can just use directly the analog been to read values from an analog sensor.

11
00:00:50,010 --> 00:00:52,920
Now, how does it work on the Andrina?

12
00:00:53,310 --> 00:00:59,430
When you read data from an analog input pin, you will get an integer value between zero and one thousand

13
00:00:59,430 --> 00:01:00,010
twenty three.

14
00:01:00,390 --> 00:01:04,830
This number will be determined directly from the voltage applied to the pin.

15
00:01:05,190 --> 00:01:11,580
So an analog sensor like you put on Showier, which is basically a valuable resistance, will simply

16
00:01:11,580 --> 00:01:16,770
apply a different voltage on the other being from zero to five volt.

17
00:01:17,240 --> 00:01:23,300
The Orange Bowl will read this voltage and translate it into an integer number.

18
00:01:23,610 --> 00:01:26,520
So four zero volt you will get the value.

19
00:01:26,720 --> 00:01:30,920
Zero or five volt you will get one thousand twenty three.

20
00:01:31,230 --> 00:01:34,170
And let's say that the voltage is three volts.

21
00:01:34,380 --> 00:01:39,870
So three volts is 60 percent of five volt, which means that you will reach the value.

22
00:01:40,050 --> 00:01:41,790
Six hundred and fourteen.

23
00:01:42,090 --> 00:01:48,630
Any other value between zero and five volt will give you a value between zero and one thousand twenty

24
00:01:48,630 --> 00:01:50,550
three in your program.

25
00:01:50,550 --> 00:01:56,650
You can directly use these range of integers, which will be easier to process than voltage numbers.

26
00:01:57,270 --> 00:02:00,590
So that's how analog input beings work.

27
00:02:01,230 --> 00:02:03,900
And now this is an important addition.

28
00:02:04,110 --> 00:02:10,050
And it may seem weird, but you can't use the analog right function on an analog being.

29
00:02:10,500 --> 00:02:17,700
The analog right function is used on only some digital pins which have a B.W. and functionality in order

30
00:02:17,700 --> 00:02:20,970
to right an analog value to actually do so.

31
00:02:20,970 --> 00:02:26,640
The choice of the name of the function from the developers of the original library was maybe not the

32
00:02:26,640 --> 00:02:28,200
best choice for clarity.

33
00:02:28,500 --> 00:02:33,120
Just remember that you can't use analog right on things.

34
00:02:33,360 --> 00:02:35,810
You can only read analog values.

35
00:02:36,150 --> 00:02:37,070
You can't write.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,180 --> 00:00:06,810
Now that you understand how analog input pins work, let's actually make some tests with our newly installed

2
00:00:06,810 --> 00:00:11,370
potentiometer so you can open here a new sketch on your Arduino ID.

3
00:00:11,520 --> 00:00:20,370
And, well, first things first, as always, we are going to use define for the PIN number we use for

4
00:00:20,370 --> 00:00:25,500
the potentiometer, and we are going to name it very simply, potentiometer.

5
00:00:27,380 --> 00:00:34,310
You know, all the bouquets and then the number of the pin is a two, so you can write a two.

6
00:00:34,550 --> 00:00:40,910
This is not a proper integer number, but this is something that is recognized by the Arduino.

7
00:00:41,240 --> 00:00:43,130
This is in the library.

8
00:00:43,160 --> 00:00:48,380
So you can use a zero, a one, a two until a five here for the Arduino UNO.

9
00:00:48,680 --> 00:00:51,260
It will be recognized as the PIN number.

10
00:00:51,710 --> 00:00:54,380
All right, then what do we need to do in the setup?

Well, usually what we did before is to use the PIN mode function to set up the mode so either input

00:01:00,770 --> 00:01:02,740
or output for the pin.

00:01:10,280 --> 00:01:14,270
function because they are already analog input bits.

15
00:01:14,570 --> 00:01:18,440
So the pin is already input, so the pin already has the input mode.

16
00:01:18,440 --> 00:01:20,980
So nothing to do in the setup.

17
00:01:20,990 --> 00:01:24,560
Just what I'm going to do is say that begin.

18
00:01:24,920 --> 00:01:26,510
Okay, because we are going to print.

19
00:01:27,730 --> 00:01:32,830
The value from the potentiometer on the style monitor so we can actually monitor it.

20
00:01:33,100 --> 00:01:33,520
All right.

21
00:01:33,520 --> 00:01:39,610
I know in the loop function, what we are going to do is simply to print the value from the potentiometer.

22
00:01:39,610 --> 00:01:41,560
So still that.

23
00:01:42,250 --> 00:01:43,180
Praying to them.

24
00:01:44,760 --> 00:01:50,610
And then to read the value from an analog input pin, you will simply use the function analog.

25
00:01:51,620 --> 00:02:00,030
Read with, of course, the pin number so we can replace with potentiometer pin here.

26
00:02:00,210 --> 00:02:03,020
Now close to parenthesis and the semicolon.

27
00:02:03,320 --> 00:02:09,380
And then so we don't print too fast, I'm going to add a delay of 100 milliseconds.

28
00:02:09,380 --> 00:02:14,420
So we are going to print the value from the potentiometer at about ten hertz.

29
00:02:14,630 --> 00:02:19,160
Now, as you can see, this is very, very similar to what we did with the push button.

30
00:02:19,550 --> 00:02:25,970
With the push button, we simply printed the value from the button using digital read, but with the

31
00:02:25,970 --> 00:02:30,110
potentiometer, because this is an analog input pin, we use analog rate.

32
00:02:30,110 --> 00:02:37,100
So the name of the function here is very simple and analog read will give you a value between zero and

33
00:02:37,100 --> 00:02:38,780
1023.

34
00:02:38,810 --> 00:02:39,920
Let's check that.

35
00:02:39,920 --> 00:02:46,670
So make sure now your board is correctly connected and let's upload code.

36
00:02:46,670 --> 00:02:47,450
Let's name it.

37
00:02:48,450 --> 00:02:49,380
Put on your mirror.

38
00:02:53,210 --> 00:02:58,260
And now let's open the sale monitor to see what value we have.

39
00:02:58,280 --> 00:03:04,010
And as you can see here, so we have the first value, which is 590.

40
00:03:04,520 --> 00:03:10,950
This is just because the potentiometer here is at a certain position between the minimum and the maximum.

41
00:03:10,970 --> 00:03:15,230
Now, I'm going to move it from one side here.

42
00:03:15,680 --> 00:03:18,560
So I move it and you can see the value is decreasing.

43
00:03:19,040 --> 00:03:23,180
I move it all the way up, all the way down to zero.

44
00:03:23,180 --> 00:03:29,510
And though I can move it the other way all the way up to 1023.

45
00:03:30,020 --> 00:03:36,920
So depending on your setup, the minimum and maximum value may be inverted from my setup.

46
00:03:36,920 --> 00:03:44,870
But the important thing is that you can go from 0 to 1023 and back to zero, and then you can have any

47
00:03:44,870 --> 00:03:47,060
intermediate value between them.

48
00:03:49,620 --> 00:03:57,480
And one other nice thing you can see is with this soil plot, you will be able to plot the value from

49
00:03:57,480 --> 00:04:00,060
0 to 1023.

50
00:04:01,290 --> 00:04:07,500
You can make a nice plot, so you can better visualize what is the value.

51
00:04:08,280 --> 00:04:08,880
All right.

52
00:04:09,710 --> 00:04:15,200
This is nice to have, but not really needed for what we need to do in the following of this course

53

and also for the final project.

Great.

So now you can read a value from the potentiometer.

1
00:00:00,210 --> 00:00:07,050
Actually, that's not all for another piece you have seen that you can use analog input to a range of

2
00:00:07,050 --> 00:00:13,680
values from an analog sensor, but analog things are, in fact, also digital.

3
00:00:14,700 --> 00:00:20,550
So by default, if you don't set a model for an analog pin and use the grid function, then it would

4
00:00:20,550 --> 00:00:22,590
work as an analog input pin.

5
00:00:22,590 --> 00:00:30,510
But then you can also use ping mode to choose a mode and use the pin, just like you would use a digital

6
00:00:30,510 --> 00:00:30,870
pin.

7
00:00:31,120 --> 00:00:35,280

8
00:00:35,280 --> 00:00:42,030

9
00:00:42,030 --> 00:00:42,550
did before.

10
00:00:42,780 --> 00:00:50,790
Note that for analog things like I have previously said, you can't use the same functionality are.

11
00:00:50,880 --> 00:00:53,610
In other words, the analog right function.

12
00:00:54,270 --> 00:00:58,140
Using an analog print as a digital pen can be very useful.

13
00:00:58,150 --> 00:01:05,100
For example, if you need to connect a new digital component and all your digital pins are already used.

14
00:01:05,530 --> 00:01:10,390
So what I recommend by default is to use digital pins when you have a digital component.

15
00:01:10,490 --> 00:01:18,330
OK, and I know because when you have an analog sensor by not mixing them, you will keep things clearer

16
00:01:18,330 --> 00:01:18,830
for you.

pin.

# Section 9: Practice More with Arduino Pins

1
00:00:00,300 --> 00:00:07,470
Great, you can work with the digital and analog pins of your in the world in the following lessons,

2
00:00:07,620 --> 00:00:13,680
you will add more elegance to your secrets and you will practice with a few activities on everything

3
00:00:13,680 --> 00:00:18,830
you have seen before, digital beans and other beans, priming content, etc..

4
00:00:19,230 --> 00:00:26,340
And before we dive in this practice section, here is a quick recap on digital and another beanz.

5
00:00:26,610 --> 00:00:32,150
You can also download a PDF as an additional resource to the schools.

6
00:00:32,550 --> 00:00:38,190
Keep this PDF on the side, which will help you clear any dolloped you may have when you write your

7
00:00:38,190 --> 00:00:40,260
own code for your own project.

8
00:00:40,590 --> 00:00:48,250
So to recap on the audio, we know you get 14 digital teens from number zero to 13.

9
00:00:48,660 --> 00:00:57,990
You also get six beans from the eight zero two eight five on different ideas about the layout and number

10
00:00:57,990 --> 00:01:00,060
of beans may vary.

11
00:01:00,600 --> 00:01:05,460
Now for digital beans, first you need to choose a mode for the bean.

12
00:01:05,940 --> 00:01:13,470
Output means that you will write data to an aggregator and input means that you will read data from

13
00:01:13,470 --> 00:01:14,060
the center.

14
00:01:14,520 --> 00:01:21,550
If you choose outputs, then depending on the bean, you have two choices for all digital beans.

15
00:01:21,810 --> 00:01:28,610
You can simply write a digital data, which means you set a binary state either high or low.

16
00:01:29,280 --> 00:01:35,820
If the pin has a value and functionality, you can either use it with a binary state or you can also

17
00:01:35,820 --> 00:01:40,740
send an analog value from zero to two hundred fifty five.

18
00:01:41,640 --> 00:01:43,860
So that is for the output mode.

19
00:01:44,400 --> 00:01:49,940
For the input mode of a digital pin, you would read a binary state from senseor.

20
00:01:50,430 --> 00:01:55,920
The value you would get will be either high or low, and that's it for digital beats.

21
00:01:57,000 --> 00:02:04,650
For either of these, you can decide to use the pen as an input pin, which is the default mode in order

22
00:02:04,650 --> 00:02:11,560
to read a of data from an analog sensor so that the remote is already selected for you.

23
00:02:11,820 --> 00:02:13,110
No need to set it.

24
00:02:13,380 --> 00:02:19,710
And then in your code, you will read values which are in the range between zero and one thousand twenty

25
00:02:19,710 --> 00:02:20,010
three.

26
00:02:21,190 --> 00:02:28,930
The other option you have for other things is to use them as classic digital pins, but in this case,

27
00:02:29,200 --> 00:02:34,510
don't forget to send a mode first and then use the other as a digital thing.

28
00:02:35,200 --> 00:02:35,740
All right.

29
00:02:35,740 --> 00:02:37,430
That's pretty much it for the recap.

30
00:02:37,750 --> 00:02:39,430
Now let's practice more.

00:09:24,700 --> 00:09:30,850

3

5

6

7

8

10

And basically you will need that in almost all your Arduino project.

12

With the time functionalities, you would be able to run multiple actions in parallel or in other words,

13

create a multitask project.

14

This section is super important because it will allow you to overcome one of the biggest problems that

1
00:00:00,180 --> 00:00:06,480
Let's make a very quick recap with the delay function, which you've already used quite a lot, and

2
00:00:06,480 --> 00:00:10,380
also I'm going to introduce to you the delay microsecond function.

3
00:00:10,990 --> 00:00:17,640
So the delay function, you have seen that you can use it with simply writing delay and then no.

4
00:00:17,670 --> 00:00:19,210
So let's say 1000.

5
00:00:20,010 --> 00:00:27,540
So you have one parameter, which is a number of milliseconds when the execution Ritchies, this function

6
00:00:27,540 --> 00:00:30,210
to delay the execution will be stopped.

7
00:00:30,480 --> 00:00:33,750
OK, well, the amount of time you have provided here.

8
00:00:34,180 --> 00:00:44,850
OK, so in this case, one second, because one seven is equal to one thousand milliseconds.

9
00:00:45,280 --> 00:00:53,190
So the minimum value you can use is one millisecond and then you can use any other value you want.

10
00:00:53,640 --> 00:00:59,940
And there is another function which is delay microseconds like this.

11
00:01:00,180 --> 00:01:06,120
They should arrange and delay microseconds is basically the same thing as delay.

12
00:01:06,360 --> 00:01:13,140
But instead of providing a number of milliseconds, you provide a number of microseconds.

13
00:01:13,680 --> 00:01:15,960
If I provide, let's say, 500.

14
00:01:17,610 --> 00:01:29,220
I have half a millisecond, because one measly signal is equal to one thousand microseconds in a microsecond

15
00:01:29,220 --> 00:01:32,170
can be represented by those letters.

16
00:01:32,670 --> 00:01:39,600
So, for example, if I do daily microsecond with one thousand, that is the same as if I do delay,

17
00:01:39,630 --> 00:01:40,860
we've won, OK?

18
00:01:41,340 --> 00:01:49,170
And now if I want to make a delay of one signal, I need to give 1000 milliseconds and I need to give

19
00:01:49,200 --> 00:01:50,560
one million.

20
00:01:50,580 --> 00:01:56,590
So with six zeroes, one million or delay microseconds.

21
00:01:57,270 --> 00:02:02,620
So the delay microseconds can be very useful when you need to wait just a few microseconds.

22
00:02:02,640 --> 00:02:10,140
OK, because the minimum you can wait for delay is one millisecond, which is actually quite a lot in

23
00:02:10,140 --> 00:02:11,060
the computer time.

24
00:02:11,920 --> 00:02:19,050
Let's say, for example, you need to wait for 15 microseconds when you read in a specific sense, for

25
00:02:19,050 --> 00:02:21,990
example, this function will be very useful.

26
00:02:22,740 --> 00:02:23,090
All right.

27
00:02:23,100 --> 00:02:29,940
So to pull the program on how do we know you have two options, the delay and the delay microseconds

28
00:02:29,940 --> 00:02:31,380
functions which delay.

29
00:02:31,380 --> 00:02:34,280
You give milliseconds and we delay microseconds.

30
00:02:34,290 --> 00:02:36,150
Of course, you need microseconds.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,330 --> 00:00:03,690
So now what is the problem with delay?

2
00:00:04,140 --> 00:00:10,400
We're going to come back to the solution of the previous activity when we used the cell communication.

3
00:00:11,140 --> 00:00:17,160
So that is activity number eight going to open that code here.

4
00:00:18,630 --> 00:00:19,810
And go back to here.

5
00:00:19,830 --> 00:00:25,740
So I have provided a few explanation already on the solution for the activity, but I'm going to give

6
00:00:25,740 --> 00:00:30,420
more details now than I understand clearly what DeLay does.

7
00:00:30,870 --> 00:00:36,660
DeLay will simply stop the execution of the program for a certain amount of time.

8
00:00:37,350 --> 00:00:43,380
And in this program, you have seen that when we are in the delay, well, we can't actually do anything

9
00:00:43,380 --> 00:00:43,840
else.

10
00:00:43,860 --> 00:00:46,380
OK, so here we decided communication.

11
00:00:46,710 --> 00:00:52,680
When you send cellular data from your computer to the you know, the hardware of the area will actually

12
00:00:52,700 --> 00:00:56,520
receive the data and it will be ready to be passed.

13
00:00:56,580 --> 00:00:59,310
OK, but because you use a delay.

14
00:00:59,700 --> 00:01:07,240
Well, if you use a delay for here four thousand milliseconds, then the thing is that if you receive

15
00:01:07,500 --> 00:01:13,740
any data before the end of the delay, you will have to wait for the end of the delay to actually be

16
00:01:13,740 --> 00:01:15,390
able to process the data.

17
00:01:15,780 --> 00:01:21,960
So what does this means is that if you use the delay function, basically you can just do one thing

18
00:01:21,960 --> 00:01:22,440
at a time.

19
00:01:23,100 --> 00:01:30,180
If you try to do two things like here blink an energy and the data from the sale, well, you can say

20
00:01:30,180 --> 00:01:36,330
you have a timing problem because you are not able to read the data as soon as it arrives, at least

21
00:01:36,540 --> 00:01:38,990
with very small timing delay.

22
00:01:39,570 --> 00:01:45,730
And there are many examples where the delay function will block you from doing what you want.

23
00:01:45,750 --> 00:01:54,000
For example, let's say you want to make two blinks, OK, here we have one blink, but let's say you

24
00:01:54,000 --> 00:01:57,530
want to make two energy blinks at different rates.

25
00:01:58,110 --> 00:02:04,120
So the first lady, for example, with blink every four hundred milliseconds and the signal that you

26
00:02:04,170 --> 00:02:06,990
will blink every eight seconds.

27
00:02:07,390 --> 00:02:09,480
OK, how can you actually do this?

28
00:02:09,480 --> 00:02:16,590
Because if you blink every eight seconds, it means you have to write a delay of eight seconds and you

29
00:02:16,590 --> 00:02:23,490
have to block the program for eight seconds, which means you are going to be so late when reading the

30
00:02:23,490 --> 00:02:29,330
cell, you might even miss some data, which means also that you can't make the other blink every one

31
00:02:29,340 --> 00:02:30,240
hundred milliseconds.

32
00:02:30,720 --> 00:02:38,280
So the only solution, if you want to keep using delay, is to split the program in many different parts,

33
00:02:38,450 --> 00:02:43,460
OK, with more delays and that will quickly become a huge mess.

34
00:02:43,980 --> 00:02:51,720
And another example, let's say you have one entity that is blinking every two seconds and then you

35
00:02:51,720 --> 00:02:55,040
want to measure some data from a census.

36
00:02:55,040 --> 00:03:00,080
So, for example, you want to measure the distance from an object with another sensor.

37
00:03:00,090 --> 00:03:02,690
That's what we are going to do in the following of these schools.

38
00:03:03,180 --> 00:03:10,020
And if the object is to close from the sensor, you are going to put on another energy to make sure

39
00:03:10,020 --> 00:03:14,070
that the user knows that there is an obstacle in the way.

40
00:03:14,430 --> 00:03:21,690
The thing is that with the energy that blinks and things, well, you would only be able to read the

41
00:03:21,690 --> 00:03:23,340
sensor every two seconds.

42
00:03:23,730 --> 00:03:31,020
So it means you will be late by telling the user that there is an obstacle and that can be a real problem

43
00:03:31,020 --> 00:03:31,870
for your application.

44
00:03:32,280 --> 00:03:34,160
So that's just a few examples.

45
00:03:34,620 --> 00:03:41,520
And if you already have tried to make programs by yourself with more than just one thing in your program,

46
00:03:41,820 --> 00:03:48,410
OK, we have multiple tasks, then you may have seen also that you are quite limiting by using this

47
00:03:48,450 --> 00:03:55,470
delay function, because the thing is that on an hourly no, it's not like a normal computer when you

48
00:03:55,470 --> 00:04:01,180
can have what we call multi training and that you can have multiple actions that run in parallel.

49
00:04:01,200 --> 00:04:02,870
So that would not be a problem here.

50
00:04:03,180 --> 00:04:05,520
You have just one action at the time.

51
00:04:05,790 --> 00:04:09,180
You can only execute one instruction at a time.

52
00:04:09,510 --> 00:04:10,920
So if you block, we delay.

53
00:04:11,340 --> 00:04:13,450
Anything else can't be executed.

54
00:04:13,950 --> 00:04:15,480
So that is the problem.

55
00:04:15,810 --> 00:04:22,830
And the good news is that there is a solution to that using other time functionalities of the idea that

56
00:04:22,830 --> 00:04:24,300
we are going to see right now.

So now what we can do with this function?

long.

Milice.

First, you can see I use unsigned, OK, but what is unsigned?

19
00:01:17,130 --> 00:01:23,040
So unsigned goes with another data type, for example, long, unsigned, int and state, long, etc.

20
00:01:23,460 --> 00:01:27,440
Unsigned simply means that all the numbers would be positive.

21
00:01:27,570 --> 00:01:31,290
So they arranged for a data type which starts at zero.

22
00:01:32,370 --> 00:01:36,810
If you remember all along we have minus.

23
00:01:36,870 --> 00:01:43,640
So we can go from minus two billion, approximately two plus two billion.

24
00:01:43,780 --> 00:01:45,880
OK, that is the range of the law.

25
00:01:46,380 --> 00:01:47,930
You can go in a negative number.

26
00:01:47,940 --> 00:01:50,850
You can go in a positive number, up to two million.

27
00:01:51,420 --> 00:01:53,010
Now, if you add unsigned.

28
00:01:55,220 --> 00:02:02,390
The along what it will do, it will shift in the numbers to all the negative number, you can't get

29
00:02:02,390 --> 00:02:05,750
them, but instead it will be added to the maximum number.

00:02:06,080 --> 00:02:07,550
So you will start at zero.

31
00:02:07,970 --> 00:02:11,660
Up to plus will be approximately.

32
00:02:11,960 --> 00:02:14,150
OK, so here why do we use unsign long?

33
00:02:14,150 --> 00:02:19,880
Because, well, time in Alvino starts at zero and then it continues to increment.

34
00:02:19,910 --> 00:02:22,120
So we don't need to start any negative number, OK.

The time will never be negative when you get with Mitty's.

So we use unsigned long and now.

00:02:29,690 --> 00:02:33,230

41
00:02:49,130 --> 00:02:56,500
or meaningful function as well, if you see that the name turns around are for example, green or blue,

117
00:08:35,500 --> 00:08:41,860
OK, so plus equals simply means that you are going to add this to the variable on the left.

118
00:08:42,700 --> 00:08:46,790
So I simply add the time interval to the previous time.

119
00:08:46,870 --> 00:08:53,470
OK, so whenever we execute the action, I'm going to add plus five hundred, which means that all the

120
00:08:53,470 --> 00:08:59,800
small delays that we have added when actually executed some code will not be taken into account.

128
00:09:33,290 --> 00:09:39,190
So to recap, first, you create an unsigned, long previous time for the action.

129
00:09:39,970 --> 00:09:44,260

130
00:09:44,380 --> 00:09:48,960

131
00:09:49,040 --> 00:09:53,290
Five hundred milliseconds, then in the loop function, you get the time.

132
00:09:53,890 --> 00:09:55,120
Time now with Milice.

133
00:09:55,540 --> 00:10:02,740

134
00:10:02,740 --> 00:10:03,890
time you did the action.

135
00:10:03,940 --> 00:10:05,470
This gives you a duration.

136
00:10:06,500 --> 00:10:13,880
You check if the direction is greater than the time interval, you have said, if, yes, you do direction

137
00:10:14,450 --> 00:10:20,960
and then you add the time interval to the previous time, which means that you are only going to go

138
00:10:20,960 --> 00:10:26,540
in if again after the time interval has passed another time.

139
00:10:27,140 --> 00:10:27,520

7
00:00:27,300 --> 00:00:34,490

00:00:47,160 --> 00:00:47,890
The button is low.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,180 --> 00:00:05,370
Now that you have seen the Bonds problem happen in a real application, I'm going to explain to you

2
00:00:05,370 --> 00:00:06,740
why we have this problem.

3
00:00:07,230 --> 00:00:10,050
Let's have a look at the signal from the button.

4
00:00:10,210 --> 00:00:16,050
OK, let's say the signal is low first and then you press the button and everything goes well.

5
00:00:16,200 --> 00:00:17,670
The signal will go higher.

6
00:00:17,760 --> 00:00:21,250
And you know that the button has been priced when you monitor it.

7
00:00:21,780 --> 00:00:27,060
Now, when you release the button, if everything goes well, the signal will go to low.

8
00:00:27,210 --> 00:00:30,190
And that's quite what you would expect from the button.

9
00:00:30,260 --> 00:00:36,110
OK, and that's what you had in most of the cases in the previous experimentation.

10
00:00:36,300 --> 00:00:40,170
So you press the button again, the signal goes to high and then you release.

11
00:00:40,170 --> 00:00:47,040
And what is happening here, as you can see when you release the button, this time the signal is bouncing

12
00:00:47,040 --> 00:00:52,760
from low to high and high to low a few times before finally going to low.

13
00:00:53,190 --> 00:00:59,340
It can happen here when you release the button, as in this example, but it can also happen when you

14
00:00:59,340 --> 00:01:06,900
press it so easy on this part of the signal, either when you realize or when you press it.

15
00:01:07,200 --> 00:01:10,380
So this is what happens when the button bounces.

16
00:01:10,740 --> 00:01:14,440
This is purely a physical issue from the button sensor.

17
00:01:14,800 --> 00:01:21,060
OK, when you press the button, the hardware part that connects the wire together can bounce a few

18
00:01:21,060 --> 00:01:22,620
times before being stable.

19
00:01:23,190 --> 00:01:25,560
That's when you read the button state in your code.

20
00:01:25,800 --> 00:01:32,880
You also see these buttons and the number of buttons is going very K can be zero bouncing in a good

21
00:01:32,880 --> 00:01:38,970
situation or just one, two, three, etc. and this can lead to behavior you don't want.

22
00:01:39,360 --> 00:01:44,190
For example, if you want to increase a counter whenever you release the button, then we have to bounce.

23
00:01:44,430 --> 00:01:50,070
The content may be incremented by more than one every time you release the button, which makes your

24
00:01:50,070 --> 00:01:52,320
application not working correctly.

25
00:01:52,770 --> 00:01:57,060
Or if you want to toggle an entity, which is an application we do later in the section.

26
00:01:57,420 --> 00:01:59,480
And I just talked about it a little bit before.

27
00:01:59,640 --> 00:02:04,520
Well, imagine that the button bounces three times like here on the screen.

28
00:02:05,010 --> 00:02:10,740
In this case, after being told a few times very quickly, the state of the editing would be the same

29
00:02:10,740 --> 00:02:12,750
as it was before you released the button.

30
00:02:13,500 --> 00:02:18,540
Note that there are some applications where you don't really need to worry about the button bouncing.

31
00:02:18,870 --> 00:02:24,480
For example, in the previous activities, we have made a project where an entity will be pulled on

32
00:02:24,630 --> 00:02:27,270
if the button is priced and proud of.

33
00:02:27,340 --> 00:02:32,910
If the button is not pressed, well, the buttons will still happen, but it will not modify the behavior

34
00:02:32,940 --> 00:02:33,840
of your project.

35
00:02:34,050 --> 00:02:40,170
The real problem is when you need to do something and the trigger for that is the change on the signal,

36
00:02:40,380 --> 00:02:42,110
not the signal itself.

37
00:02:42,420 --> 00:02:45,330
Fortunately, there is a solution to that issue.

38
00:02:45,780 --> 00:02:50,430
By modifying the code a little bit, we will be able to remove the balance effect.

39
00:02:50,800 --> 00:02:52,410
The principle is quite easy.

40
00:02:52,710 --> 00:02:58,620
After we noticed that there is a change of state, we are going to simply ignore all of the changes

41
00:02:58,620 --> 00:02:59,910
for a given amount of time.

42
00:03:00,280 --> 00:03:05,820
So here on the diagram, you can see that when we have the balance problem, if we ignore all changes

43
00:03:05,820 --> 00:03:11,460
for this amount of time here with the Green Arrow, then we don't take into account all the bounces

44
00:03:11,460 --> 00:03:12,000
that happen.

45
00:03:12,720 --> 00:03:17,220
The period during which we ignore changes is called the DBMS delay.

46
00:03:17,820 --> 00:03:22,890
In your application, you will be able to choose to increase or decrease the delay so you can make sure

47
00:03:22,890 --> 00:03:28,070
you don't get any bumps in your code, but also that you don't miss any real change in the signal.

48
00:03:28,470 --> 00:03:34,170
And of course, to ignore the changes for the short duration, we are not going to use the delay function,

49
00:03:34,530 --> 00:03:37,750
but instead the time functionalities that you have just discovered.

50
00:03:38,340 --> 00:03:41,820
Now let's see how to write the code for this Diebold's functionality.

# Section 13: Arduino Interrupts

1
00:00:00,210 --> 00:00:06,030
In the following license, you will learn how to use the interrupts functionality you have finished

2
00:00:06,030 --> 00:00:12,030
solving the pushbutton problem, which means that from now on you would be able to use the button to

3
00:00:12,030 --> 00:00:15,540
trigger some action only when the state of the button changes.

4
00:00:15,840 --> 00:00:22,770
For example, when you press authorities the button and actually we are not totally done with this push

5
00:00:22,770 --> 00:00:23,120
button.

6
00:00:23,580 --> 00:00:28,730
This component is one of the most important ones which you will use in many of your project.

7
00:00:29,070 --> 00:00:32,250
So it's a good thing to know the different ways to handle it.

8
00:00:32,730 --> 00:00:36,250
Now it's time to learn about our new interrupts.

9
00:00:36,750 --> 00:00:43,530
I will first explain what are interrupts our interruptions, and then you will use them with the pushbutton

10
00:00:43,740 --> 00:00:49,620
and combine them with the balance functionality you have just discovered at the end of the section.

11
00:00:49,800 --> 00:00:56,670
You mean ready know everything you need in order to correctly handle those buttons in any of your future

12
00:00:56,760 --> 00:00:57,330
projects.

13
00:00:57,690 --> 00:00:58,810
And let's get started.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,330 --> 00:00:07,320
And before we jump into the code, what is how do to interrupt our interaction to understand interrupt?

2
00:00:07,500 --> 00:00:09,930
First, let's see a real life example.

3
00:00:10,710 --> 00:00:16,190
Let's say you are waiting for a package to arrive at your home in order to not meet the package.

4
00:00:16,200 --> 00:00:17,910
You have two choices of action.

5
00:00:18,450 --> 00:00:21,970
First, you can come back every minute to your front door to check in.

6
00:00:21,990 --> 00:00:24,120
The delivery truck is here or not.

7
00:00:24,450 --> 00:00:28,440
And that is called pulling all you can do something else.

8
00:00:28,680 --> 00:00:35,550
And when you hear the doorbell, you interrupt your current activity to open the front door and get

9
00:00:35,550 --> 00:00:36,240
the package.

10
00:00:36,660 --> 00:00:40,010
This is called interrupt or interruption.

11
00:00:40,590 --> 00:00:46,320
And when you work with a push button or a similar digital sensor, this is the same.

12
00:00:46,770 --> 00:00:52,800
You can either check the button state every time you get the change in the loop function or you can

13
00:00:52,800 --> 00:00:59,520
sit up and interrupt to post the execution of the program and call a function as soon as the button

14
00:00:59,530 --> 00:01:07,530
state has changed and interrupt will be triggered by hardware without necessitating the software resources

15
00:01:07,530 --> 00:01:08,270
of the algorithm.

16
00:01:08,760 --> 00:01:13,800
So when the interrupt is triggered, your program is paused and you interrupt.

17
00:01:13,800 --> 00:01:14,880
Function is called.

18
00:01:15,390 --> 00:01:20,600
As soon as your interact function has finished its execution, the program resumes.

19
00:01:21,090 --> 00:01:22,800
So using interrupts.

20
00:01:22,800 --> 00:01:25,620
This is bullying can be a good idea.

21
00:01:25,620 --> 00:01:32,120
For example, if you want to be notified as soon as the button is priced or released, because we've

22
00:01:32,130 --> 00:01:38,250
polling, even if your program goes very fast, you still have to wait for the loop function to finish

23
00:01:38,250 --> 00:01:45,000
out the other instructions of your application between each read of the button state, which may or

24
00:01:45,000 --> 00:01:47,600
may not be a problem in some situation.

25
00:01:47,850 --> 00:01:52,890
And also using interrupts can ensure you that you don't miss the change of the state.

26
00:01:53,460 --> 00:01:55,340
For the example of the package delivery.

27
00:01:55,560 --> 00:02:02,040
Let's say you check every minute, but then if the delivery truck comes in between that minute and just

28
00:02:02,040 --> 00:02:06,720
waits five seconds before leaving, well, you might miss your package.

29
00:02:07,300 --> 00:02:11,670
So, of course, we already know the timing is much quicker for all the action you do.

30
00:02:12,010 --> 00:02:14,460
OK, we are not talking in seconds or minutes.

31
00:02:14,460 --> 00:02:17,420
We will be talking in microseconds or milliseconds.

32
00:02:17,820 --> 00:02:23,510
And by using the time functionalities and not the delay functions, we pulling.

33
00:02:23,520 --> 00:02:27,240
You should not have the problem of missing the change of state.

34
00:02:27,750 --> 00:02:33,120
But the more your program grows and the more instructions you add, which can take some time, the more

35
00:02:33,120 --> 00:02:34,960
chance you have to miss it.

36
00:02:35,490 --> 00:02:42,150
So if what you want to do is to get notified of when the button state changed while also doing many

37
00:02:42,150 --> 00:02:45,810
other things, then using interrupts is actually a good idea.

38
00:02:46,170 --> 00:02:48,540
And actually just a small parenthesis here.

39
00:02:48,750 --> 00:02:51,380
Interrupts are not just for push buttons, ok.

40
00:02:51,900 --> 00:02:58,230
Interrupts can be used whenever you want to monitor the change of the state for a digital pin.

41
00:02:58,800 --> 00:03:02,210
Now what are the triggers for the interrupt function to be called.

42
00:03:02,670 --> 00:03:10,110
So here is a signal that starts low or zero vote and then goes high or five-fold, for example, when

43
00:03:10,110 --> 00:03:16,960
you press the button and then goes back to low when you release the button, the first mode for interrupts

44
00:03:16,980 --> 00:03:19,290
is rising with this mode.

45
00:03:19,470 --> 00:03:26,000
The interrupt will be triggered when the signal goes from low to high and only this.

46
00:03:26,400 --> 00:03:27,990
The second mode is following.

47
00:03:28,410 --> 00:03:30,150
This is the opposite here.

48
00:03:30,180 --> 00:03:34,280
The interrupt will be triggered when the signal goes from high to low.

49
00:03:34,950 --> 00:03:41,340
Then the third mode is change and is actually a combination of rising and fully.

50
00:03:42,330 --> 00:03:48,810
So if you have any of those two, we've changed, the incorrupt will be triggered and finally, default

51
00:03:48,810 --> 00:03:55,620
mode is low, this mode is a little bit different because the interrupt will be triggered whenever the

52
00:03:55,620 --> 00:03:56,700
signal is low.

53
00:03:57,000 --> 00:04:03,420
So it's quite useful when you have a signal that is high all the time and which goes to low only at

54
00:04:03,420 --> 00:04:07,170
some specific moments for a very short duration.

55
00:04:07,470 --> 00:04:10,170
But otherwise, in our case, we are not going to use it.

56
00:04:10,590 --> 00:04:13,700
OK, so to recap, those are the four modes you can choose.

57
00:04:13,710 --> 00:04:17,790
OK, we are going to see how to do that practically in the next.

58
00:04:17,790 --> 00:04:23,790
Listen, so we've rising to interrupt would be triggered when the signal goes from low to high.

59
00:04:24,210 --> 00:04:30,750
We following when the signal goes from high to low changed is the combination of rising and falling

60
00:04:31,380 --> 00:04:33,840
and low is whenever the signal is low.

61
00:04:34,530 --> 00:04:37,590
Now, what other options can you use for interrupts?

62
00:04:38,130 --> 00:04:43,320
Well, first, this is only happening with digital things, not analog bits.

63
00:04:43,920 --> 00:04:46,850
Then it depends a lot on your Arduino model.

64
00:04:47,520 --> 00:04:49,770
Here is a table for the most common ones.

65
00:04:50,400 --> 00:04:55,650
But you can see the outline of, you know, that we have or if you have the Nano or Mini, well, you

66
00:04:55,650 --> 00:04:58,790
only have two digital things you can use for Internet.

67
00:04:58,800 --> 00:05:01,230
And those are pince number two and three.

68
00:05:01,770 --> 00:05:03,210
OK, so only two things.

69
00:05:03,210 --> 00:05:08,790
If you have the Omega Omega, you can see you have much more digital things you can use for interrupts.

70
00:05:09,120 --> 00:05:15,570
OK, then you can see from micro Leonardo then if you have zero are doing, you can use all digital

71
00:05:15,570 --> 00:05:18,720
things except for the PIN number four for the Adeno Zero.

72
00:05:19,310 --> 00:05:25,100
OK, so you can see depending on your application, if you need to use many interrupts, well the algorithm

73
00:05:25,500 --> 00:05:31,980
might not be the best solution, but for what we are going to do now and for the final project of the

74
00:05:31,980 --> 00:05:36,070
course and for many of your project that you are going to start afterwards.

75
00:05:36,360 --> 00:05:37,770
The you know, is quite good.

76
00:05:37,810 --> 00:05:40,470
OK, you can already manage to do a lot of things.

77
00:05:40,470 --> 00:05:41,890
We just two interns.

78
00:05:42,210 --> 00:05:42,660
All right.

79
00:05:42,660 --> 00:05:49,360
So now you have a global understanding of what our interests are and when or why to use them.

80

to apply the new state to the atypia.

100
00:06:59,670 --> 00:07:05,610
the toggle and inside the button, really interrupt, OK, because that's what we want to do.

101
00:07:05,940 --> 00:07:09,000
But that's not what we are going to do, actually.

102
00:07:09,540 --> 00:07:10,260
Why is that?

103
00:07:10,620 --> 00:07:17,100
Because the interim function will block your program, which stopped your program, and it's also going

104
00:07:17,100 --> 00:07:20,180
to disable every other interrupt.

105
00:07:20,330 --> 00:07:25,140
OK, so you have to know that in order, you know, you can create interrupts like this.

106
00:07:25,290 --> 00:07:30,900
But there are also other interrupts that run in the background that you may not be aware of.

107
00:07:31,050 --> 00:07:36,630
But the thing is that when you create and when you are in an interim function, every other interrupt

108
00:07:36,630 --> 00:07:41,960
is disabled and your main program is completely stopped looking.

109
00:07:41,960 --> 00:07:48,210
Hear what's wrong with that is that we use digital right to write is a function to do an action and

110
00:07:48,210 --> 00:07:51,060
this will take a few microseconds.

111
00:07:51,370 --> 00:07:54,560
OK, and this is more than just changing the state.

112
00:07:54,570 --> 00:07:56,280
OK, changing variable.

113
00:07:56,580 --> 00:08:02,970
So a best practice for an interim function is just to change some variables.

114
00:08:02,970 --> 00:08:07,530
And then after you have changed some buyable, you will handle the action in the loop.

115
00:08:07,590 --> 00:08:09,510
OK, let's see how to do that.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,240 --> 00:00:06,930
So great, you have learned how to use interrupts and as a small parenthesis, how to use the Internet,

2
00:00:07,230 --> 00:00:07,680
don't know.

3
00:00:07,680 --> 00:00:12,480
Let's forget about these debates and let's fully focus on the interrupts themselves.

4
00:00:12,930 --> 00:00:14,760
So to correctly use interrupts.

5
00:00:14,940 --> 00:00:21,900
You have to be careful and aware about what you can and can't do and what you should and shouldn't do.

6
00:00:22,500 --> 00:00:27,850
Here is a quick list of the best practices and warnings that will help you when working with interests.

7
00:00:28,440 --> 00:00:35,130
So first, an interim function does not return any result and does not receive any parameter.

8
00:00:35,580 --> 00:00:41,190
So if you want to get on, modify some variables in your program, you will have to modify global variables

9
00:00:41,460 --> 00:00:43,290
inside the internal function.

10
00:00:43,710 --> 00:00:47,880
And note that for any viable you modify inside and interrupt, you should.

11
00:00:47,880 --> 00:00:56,010
But the volatile key word in front of the data type, when you declare the variable now inside to enter

12
00:00:56,010 --> 00:01:03,880
a function, you only want to write code that goes very fast to execute, for example, toggle available.

13
00:01:04,350 --> 00:01:06,510
Basically, that's all you should aim to do.

14
00:01:06,510 --> 00:01:10,550
In the interim, you should avoid doing any action in any threat.

15
00:01:11,030 --> 00:01:17,550
Just use the Interop to toggle available and then in your void loop function, check for that variable

16
00:01:17,550 --> 00:01:19,940
and execute annexion accordingly.

17
00:01:20,430 --> 00:01:27,300
If you stay too long inside an interrupt, you might miss important data you receive in your voice function.

18
00:01:27,540 --> 00:01:33,240
Or you could miss another interrupt that is deactivated while you are in the current one.

19
00:01:33,600 --> 00:01:39,120
Now, if you want to use time functionality inside and interrupt, be extra careful.

20
00:01:39,630 --> 00:01:44,160
First, the delay function won't work and delay microsurgeons.

21
00:01:44,160 --> 00:01:51,000
Wolk's but as you want to optimize the speed, you definitely don't want to intentionally wait in the

22
00:01:51,000 --> 00:01:53,750
interrupt, so don't use any of them.

23
00:01:54,180 --> 00:02:01,110
Something very useful can be to get the time at the beginning of you in 30 minutes and micros will give

24
00:02:01,110 --> 00:02:07,460
you the current time just before the entire function starts, but then inside it to interrupt function

25
00:02:07,770 --> 00:02:16,270
minutes will not increase and micros will work accurately about one or two milliseconds and that's it.

26
00:02:16,440 --> 00:02:20,460
The thing is, you don't want to spend even one millisecond on the interrupt.

27
00:02:20,850 --> 00:02:24,070
You just want to toggle some level and then exit.

28
00:02:24,690 --> 00:02:30,880
So just use Milice on micros to get the time at the beginning if you need it, but no more.

29
00:02:31,470 --> 00:02:34,440
And finally, simple doesn't work in the Interent.

30
00:02:34,950 --> 00:02:40,020
If you want to bring something when an interrupt was triggered, you will have to do it in the void

31
00:02:40,020 --> 00:02:46,710
loop function and find a way to notify the loop when to interrupt was triggered to conclude.

32
00:02:46,710 --> 00:02:53,190
You can see that there are many drawbacks and limitations about interrupt interrupt a powerful Ardino

33
00:02:53,310 --> 00:02:53,880
function.

34
00:02:54,090 --> 00:02:58,950
But to use with care, with the information you have here, you should be fine.

35
00:02:59,280 --> 00:03:05,550
And because all those limitations and things to care about, it's best to use interrupt only when you

36
00:03:05,550 --> 00:03:09,220
really need them when designing a new project.

37
00:03:09,360 --> 00:03:14,130
Always ask yourself if what you want to achieve can just be done with pulling.

38
00:03:14,620 --> 00:03:16,760
If yes, then use pulling.

39
00:03:16,950 --> 00:03:23,220
If, however, you might miss some data using, pulling or get information a little bit too late, then

40
00:03:23,370 --> 00:03:24,600
go with interrupts.

# Section 14: EEPROM - Save Values on the Arduino

1
00:00:00,180 --> 00:00:06,660
In the following lessons, you are going to learn how to store values in the memory of your AMINUL so

2
00:00:06,660 --> 00:00:12,320
that you can reuse those values later, even after you restart the program execution.

3
00:00:13,050 --> 00:00:16,230
So you have just finished to work with interrupts.

4
00:00:16,710 --> 00:00:24,150
Now, with the time functionalities, debone and interrupts, you can create a project with any number

5
00:00:24,150 --> 00:00:26,700
of tasks running at the same time.

6
00:00:27,090 --> 00:00:28,980
And we have the same communication.

7
00:00:29,220 --> 00:00:35,600
You can make your program interact with an external user to complete this second part of the course.

8
00:00:35,970 --> 00:00:41,220
We are now going to take a closer look at it from memory in this section.

9
00:00:41,490 --> 00:00:48,390
First, I would explain what is the EPROM memory, when to use it, and also what are its limitations,

10
00:00:48,630 --> 00:00:51,180
because you need to be careful when using it.

11
00:00:51,820 --> 00:00:59,280
After that, you will save your first data into the April memory and also retrieve the data after restarting

12
00:00:59,280 --> 00:00:59,910
the program.

13
00:01:00,270 --> 00:01:05,080
And of course, we would end the section with more practice to better understand the concept.

14
00:01:05,760 --> 00:01:07,070
All right, Dan, let's get started.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,150 --> 00:00:06,930
As you already know, when you start or restart an out in a program, all the data stored in the valuables

2
00:00:07,110 --> 00:00:08,470
is completely gone.

3
00:00:08,850 --> 00:00:13,560
Every time you start from scratch, it could be useful to store a value.

4
00:00:13,560 --> 00:00:17,570
For example, the setting which replaces the default one.

5
00:00:18,060 --> 00:00:22,950
Let's say you are printing the temperature from the sensor and you offer two options.

6
00:00:23,220 --> 00:00:30,540
Either you can display the temperature in degrees Celsius or in degrees Fahrenheit and you can change

7
00:00:30,540 --> 00:00:32,940
between them by pressing on the push button.

8
00:00:33,390 --> 00:00:40,020
Well, if you can't save the sitting every time we start or restart the program, you will get the same

9
00:00:40,020 --> 00:00:40,950
default setting.

10
00:00:41,310 --> 00:00:47,880
However, if you are able to save it for later, when you restart the program, you can just read what

11
00:00:47,880 --> 00:00:51,020
was the previous setting and apply it directly.

12
00:00:51,390 --> 00:00:54,150
The April memory, solve that issue.

13
00:00:54,630 --> 00:00:59,840
During the execution of the program, you can save different values to the memory.

14
00:01:00,330 --> 00:01:07,620
Those values are stored just like values would be stored on your hard drive for your computer when you

15
00:01:07,620 --> 00:01:09,010
restart your computer.

16
00:01:09,330 --> 00:01:13,050
The run is gone, but not the data on your hard drive.

17
00:01:13,770 --> 00:01:19,980
So when you restart the program, you can read the data previously stored in the EPROM and use it to

18
00:01:19,980 --> 00:01:21,190
do whatever you want.

19
00:01:21,720 --> 00:01:24,520
So the principle is quite easy to understand.

20
00:01:25,060 --> 00:01:31,240
Now, before we actually use EPROM for the first time, there are some limitations you have to know

21
00:01:31,260 --> 00:01:32,500
to correctly use it.

22
00:01:33,090 --> 00:01:37,050
First, not all Arduino boards have a memory.

23
00:01:37,470 --> 00:01:44,040
It's available on Nonno and Maiya, for example, but not on Arduino zero.

24
00:01:44,970 --> 00:01:51,330
Then the storage is super limited on untrainable such as window and nano.

25
00:01:51,630 --> 00:01:59,220
You can only store one thousand twenty four values, each one being about between zero and two hundred

26
00:01:59,520 --> 00:02:00,260
fifty five.

27
00:02:00,690 --> 00:02:04,140
So that's a very small memory on average.

28
00:02:04,150 --> 00:02:07,890
Amiga, it's full time, bigger, but still very small.

29
00:02:08,670 --> 00:02:15,780
And one important thing to always remember on a given address of the EPROM, you only have about one

30
00:02:15,780 --> 00:02:18,060
hundred thousand writing cycles.

31
00:02:18,630 --> 00:02:26,940
You can read Evalu as many times as you want, but when you write to Epuron after 100000 writing operations

32
00:02:26,940 --> 00:02:34,180
on a given address, this address will be kind of weird off and it won't work anymore.

33
00:02:34,980 --> 00:02:38,850
So from all those points, we can make some conclusions.

34
00:02:39,510 --> 00:02:44,480
EPROM with only be useful to store very small values and not too often.

35
00:02:44,970 --> 00:02:51,780
The best example is a default setting you want to override with the previous sitting from the last execution

36
00:02:51,780 --> 00:02:53,000
of your Arduino program.

37
00:02:53,640 --> 00:03:00,990
Don't expect to store things like photos or videos or even text larger than, let's say, two pages.

38
00:03:01,440 --> 00:03:08,850
And then due to the limitation of the writing cycles, never, ever write in your EPROM at full speed

39
00:03:09,060 --> 00:03:15,960
in the void look function of your program or in any other infinite loop that goes very fast, you would

40
00:03:15,960 --> 00:03:21,170
quickly reach the one hundred thousand limit and then the address can't be used anymore.

41
00:03:21,750 --> 00:03:28,680
So test your program before if you are not sure and if you correctly test your program before writing

42
00:03:29,010 --> 00:03:31,980
it from memory, well, you will not have any problem.
