All right, and that's the end of the security as well as the section on April.

185
00:14:27,180 --> 00:14:31,030
So the important thing is make sure you don't write at full speed.

186
00:14:31,170 --> 00:14:34,740
OK, but if you correctly test your program, you should be fine.

187
00:14:34,920 --> 00:14:40,950
And as you can see, that's a quite powerful way of setting a default value and keeping this default

188
00:14:40,950 --> 00:14:43,850
value for multiple runs of your program.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 15: Ultrasonic Sensor - Measure Distances

1
00:00:00,180 --> 00:00:07,410
In the following lessons, you will learn how to detect a distance with you and we know using an ultrasonic

2
00:00:07,410 --> 00:00:07,980
sensor.

3
00:00:08,580 --> 00:00:14,940
But first, congratulations, you have just finished the second part of the course where you learned

4
00:00:14,940 --> 00:00:17,480
more interesting functionalities.

5
00:00:17,700 --> 00:00:19,940
Now you have a very good foundation.

6
00:00:20,250 --> 00:00:26,640
Those functionalities will help you create more complex project and understand how to work with different

7
00:00:26,640 --> 00:00:27,690
hardware components.

8
00:00:27,930 --> 00:00:33,920
And now let's start the third part of the class, and this part will be quite fun.

9
00:00:34,230 --> 00:00:38,540
You will experiment with different hardware, sensors and actuators.

10
00:00:38,910 --> 00:00:43,110
Those components are a bit more complex than the classic and is, for example.

11
00:00:43,410 --> 00:00:48,390
But they will also allow you to discover very useful functionalities.

12
00:00:48,630 --> 00:00:53,520
We will start with the ultrasonic sensor with which you can measure distances.

13
00:00:53,820 --> 00:01:01,260
Then you will use an LCD screen to get some Real-Time text feedback directly on your Arduino secret.

14
00:01:01,650 --> 00:01:07,300
You will discover how to control your of application with an infrared remote controller.

15
00:01:07,590 --> 00:01:13,740
And finally, how to get the luminosity of the environment using a photoresistor sensor.

16
00:01:13,920 --> 00:01:20,790
And because the whole course is built with a step by step approach where each building block leads to

17
00:01:20,790 --> 00:01:27,090
another, you will see that with those components we will use all the concepts, tools and techniques

18
00:01:27,330 --> 00:01:28,760
that we have seen so far.

19
00:01:29,100 --> 00:01:30,200
You will practice more.

20
00:01:30,210 --> 00:01:37,500
We've secured building programming, digital and beans, cell time functionalities, multitasking,

21
00:01:37,500 --> 00:01:38,970
interrupts, etc..

22
00:01:39,510 --> 00:01:45,360
So before you start this part of the course, make sure you have correctly finished the previous spots.

23
00:01:45,780 --> 00:01:47,520
But don't worry too much.

24
00:01:47,520 --> 00:01:53,280
If you don't have a 100 percent understanding of everything right now, that's not really a problem.

25
00:01:53,460 --> 00:01:59,430
You will better understand all the previous concepts in this new part and during the following sections.

26
00:01:59,640 --> 00:02:05,760
If you still have any doubt, come back to the previous sections to refresh your memory on a concept.

27
00:02:06,070 --> 00:02:13,920
Rewash the explanations are also redo the practice exercises and after finishing to explore those new

28
00:02:13,920 --> 00:02:20,460
hardware components, you will have finished the third part of the course and you will work on the final

29
00:02:20,460 --> 00:02:21,090
project.

30
00:02:21,390 --> 00:02:23,620
As you can see, we are almost there.

31
00:02:24,300 --> 00:02:30,510
So now let's focus on the first section of this new part, the ultrasonic sensor.

32
00:02:31,080 --> 00:02:37,230
In this section, you will add the ultrasonic sensor to your secretes and then discover how to control

33
00:02:37,230 --> 00:02:42,840
the sensor so you can measure a distance between the sensor and an object or a wall.

34
00:02:43,050 --> 00:02:48,300
With some activities, you will improve the way to read the distance using interns.

35
00:02:48,480 --> 00:02:55,170
And you would also practice by using EDS and ultrasonic sensor in a single application.

36
00:02:55,320 --> 00:02:56,250
And let's start.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,180 --> 00:00:05,940
Now that you have added ultrasonics insult to your in a secret, let's understand a few things about

2
00:00:05,940 --> 00:00:12,630
it and how it works so we can better understand then how to control it in the original code.

3
00:00:12,870 --> 00:00:15,240
So what does the ultrasonic sensor do?

4
00:00:15,510 --> 00:00:21,120
Well, it will measure a distance from an object on a wall that is in front of the sensor.

5
00:00:21,330 --> 00:00:31,080
OK, so here we are using the HTC as our four sensor and here are the practical characteristics of the

6
00:00:31,080 --> 00:00:31,550
sensor.

7
00:00:31,570 --> 00:00:36,530
So the characteristic you need to know in order to use it correctly.

8
00:00:36,960 --> 00:00:43,710
So the range of the sensor is between two centimetre and 400 centimeter, which means that, as you

9
00:00:43,710 --> 00:00:51,540
can see here on this diagram, you can start measuring objects and obstacles from two centimeters away

10
00:00:51,540 --> 00:01:00,810
from the sensor up to about four meter here, which is about one hundred and fifty something inches.

11
00:01:00,900 --> 00:01:09,930
If you need the conversion to whatever is to close from the sensor, less than two centimeter will not

12
00:01:09,930 --> 00:01:14,260
be measured and whatever is too far will not be measured either.

13
00:01:14,530 --> 00:01:18,630
OK, then you have the measuring angle, which is 15 degrees.

14
00:01:18,670 --> 00:01:22,340
OK, so on this diagram, it's not exactly 15 degrees.

15
00:01:22,370 --> 00:01:26,280
OK, just so you can understand, but you can see the angle.

16
00:01:26,280 --> 00:01:32,590
So everything which is below that angle or above that angle will not be measured.

17
00:01:32,970 --> 00:01:37,830
So the sensor, as you can see, will measure mostly what is in front of it.

18
00:01:37,860 --> 00:01:44,500
OK, you don't need to remember 15 degrees, just that the sensor will measure what is in front of it.

19
00:01:44,520 --> 00:01:46,420
OK, not on the side of the sensor.

20
00:01:46,500 --> 00:01:52,050
OK, mostly what's in front and from two centimeter to four meters.

21
00:01:52,350 --> 00:01:59,400
And so it's not uncommon in a robot, for example, a mobile robot to have multiple of those sensors.

22
00:01:59,580 --> 00:02:04,950
OK, we can have different sensors for different angles of the robots and different positions.

23
00:02:05,420 --> 00:02:07,680
Now, how does it work?

24
00:02:07,680 --> 00:02:08,460
Very quickly.

25
00:02:09,060 --> 00:02:12,160
So you don't need to understand everything that is written here.

26
00:02:12,930 --> 00:02:16,430
This is just the diagram from the sensor data sheet.

27
00:02:16,800 --> 00:02:19,110
So here, what are you going to do first?

28
00:02:19,130 --> 00:02:23,860
You have seen that we have a trigger in your trigger digital.

29
00:02:24,610 --> 00:02:31,500
What you are going to do to start measuring the distance is you're going to send so the signal will

30
00:02:31,510 --> 00:02:32,580
be low by default.

31
00:02:32,940 --> 00:02:41,880
You are going to send a high signal or 10 microseconds and then look after the ultrasonic sensor will

32
00:02:41,880 --> 00:02:45,060
be triggered and thus will start to measure the distance.

33
00:02:45,270 --> 00:02:48,710
OK, this is an active measurement, not a passive one.

34
00:02:49,050 --> 00:02:56,040
So because this is active, what the ultrasonic sensor will do, it will send burst, OK, with some

35
00:02:56,040 --> 00:02:57,540
wave or some pulse.

36
00:02:57,780 --> 00:03:07,200
It will send it in front of its OK to the range that is in front of it and then it will just wait until

37
00:03:07,200 --> 00:03:09,570
it receives some pulse back.

38
00:03:09,670 --> 00:03:16,530
OK, so if there is an object in front of the sensor, the wave will bounce on the object and will be

39
00:03:16,530 --> 00:03:17,760
returned to the sensor.

40
00:03:18,120 --> 00:03:25,460
OK, so the sensor will measure the duration of how long the wave takes to come back to the sensor.

41
00:03:25,860 --> 00:03:33,110
And then once it has measured this, then you will get some data on the echo pin.

42
00:03:33,270 --> 00:03:40,770
OK, so you have the training and equipping so the equipment will be low by default and then the ultrasonic

43
00:03:40,770 --> 00:03:44,730
sensor will simply but this thing.

44
00:03:44,740 --> 00:03:46,800
So you will read this as an input.

45
00:03:47,040 --> 00:03:53,130
It will put this being high for a certain duration and then low and these duration will be proportional

46
00:03:53,130 --> 00:03:55,050
to the distance of the object.

47
00:03:55,260 --> 00:04:02,190
OK, so if the waves from the sensor comes back very quickly, then the time is quite quick and this

48
00:04:02,190 --> 00:04:04,140
pulse would be quite quick.

49
00:04:04,170 --> 00:04:13,740
Also, if the object is very far at the maximum distance, then these bursts will be longer and the

50
00:04:13,740 --> 00:04:19,710
exact computation for how to measure the distance from the duration here we are going to see that in

51
00:04:19,710 --> 00:04:20,090
the code.

52
00:04:20,730 --> 00:04:21,810
So to quickly, a record.

53
00:04:22,440 --> 00:04:25,920
As a practical recap, you only need to do two things.

54
00:04:25,920 --> 00:04:33,690
First, to send a 10 microsecond high signal on the trigger pin that we are going to do with data.

55
00:04:33,690 --> 00:04:34,020
Right.

56
00:04:34,650 --> 00:04:41,720
And then so this you just need to know it exists, but then you need to simply monitor the echo pin,

57
00:04:41,760 --> 00:04:49,020
OK, and measure the duration of that puts so that ECOFIN will be digital being set as input.

58
00:04:49,320 --> 00:04:52,110
But then how can we measure the duration of that.

59
00:04:52,110 --> 00:04:56,670
But this we are going to use the pulse in function.

60
00:04:57,070 --> 00:04:59,430
OK, and I'm just going to describe quickly.

61
00:04:59,690 --> 00:05:07,940
How does the policing function works so here, this signal here is that one, OK, so it is low and

62
00:05:07,940 --> 00:05:09,740
then it is high and then it is low.

63
00:05:10,040 --> 00:05:11,130
This is a pulse.

OK, it's not because it's working fine in simulation that is going to work fine in real life.

14
00:00:55,530 --> 00:01:01,800
And it's also not because it's working fine in real life, once or twice that every time you do the

15
00:01:01,800 --> 00:01:04,550
same setup, it's going to work fine also.

16
00:01:05,010 --> 00:01:05,290
Right.

17
00:01:05,320 --> 00:01:07,740
That's one of the main issue with hardware.

18
00:01:07,750 --> 00:01:08,670
It's not really an issue.

19
00:01:08,670 --> 00:01:09,910
It's just how it works.

20
00:01:10,410 --> 00:01:14,480
So you have to be prepared to face that kind of issue in many of your project.

21
00:01:14,490 --> 00:01:17,380
But that's a normal part of any project.

22
00:01:17,700 --> 00:01:23,550
So if you have a problem with how do it, you can either fix the hardware and sometimes you can also

23
00:01:23,550 --> 00:01:25,950
try to create a fix with software.

24
00:01:26,130 --> 00:01:32,320
OK, so here I'm going to talk about both and not that it's also a little bit more advanced, OK?

25
00:01:32,340 --> 00:01:34,710
This is, again, not a big you.

26
00:01:34,920 --> 00:01:38,540
Listen, this is more of an intermediate listen.

27
00:01:39,060 --> 00:01:42,820
So what could go wrong with the hardware?

28
00:01:43,050 --> 00:01:48,590
First, I have shown you that maybe the connections on your breadboard and your arduino are not correct.

29
00:01:48,600 --> 00:01:55,640
So make sure you check that 100 percent, maybe change some wires, change some connectors, connect

30
00:01:55,650 --> 00:01:59,000
the sensor to a different place to see if you can solve the issue.

31
00:01:59,210 --> 00:02:04,920
OK, if it doesn't work well, maybe it's simply because you have the bad quality sensor.

32
00:02:05,190 --> 00:02:10,800
And actually, if you have a starter kit like the starter kit that I have that I'm using right now,

33
00:02:11,280 --> 00:02:18,060
if you have both the cheapest sensor you can find when the thing is that it depends a lot on the manufacturer

34
00:02:18,060 --> 00:02:19,320
of the hardware component.

35
00:02:19,370 --> 00:02:24,990
OK, if you buy the cheapest one, you can maybe expect that disordering of the components on the sensor

36
00:02:24,990 --> 00:02:31,440
is not well made, that maybe some parts that needs to be aligned are not completely aligned.

37
00:02:31,780 --> 00:02:38,790
OK, that's the firmware running on the sensor is maybe not a good quality one either and many different

38
00:02:38,790 --> 00:02:39,210
things.

39
00:02:39,210 --> 00:02:40,320
OK, that can go wrong.

40
00:02:40,410 --> 00:02:46,350
So basically when you buy cheap hardware, well, don't expect it to work 100 percent of the time.

41
00:02:46,540 --> 00:02:52,620
So if you have the chance to get another ultrasonic sensor, maybe it's better to try the second one

42
00:02:52,620 --> 00:02:53,300
if you have one.

43
00:02:53,640 --> 00:02:58,320
And in the future, whenever you want to buy hardware component, if it's very, very, very cheap,

44
00:02:58,320 --> 00:03:00,240
I advise you maybe to buy two or three.

45
00:03:00,610 --> 00:03:05,440
OK, so in case you have one that is not working correctly, you can try the other one.

46
00:03:05,610 --> 00:03:11,520
So if you think it is working well and your component is a bad quality, well, the only option you

47
00:03:11,520 --> 00:03:12,480
have is to change.

48
00:03:12,480 --> 00:03:17,720
The components are also maybe to try to make a fix from the software, which we are going to do here,

49
00:03:17,820 --> 00:03:22,440
and also a third cause that maybe is simply the environment.

50
00:03:22,440 --> 00:03:24,990
OK, where are you operating the sensor?

51
00:03:25,290 --> 00:03:30,950
Because DICENZO is actually sending ultrasonic waves in the environment.

52
00:03:31,200 --> 00:03:36,330
So for my specific example, I am actually in a winning recording here.

53
00:03:36,510 --> 00:03:42,720
I am in a small room with a lot of glass walls and a lot of stuff in the wall.

54
00:03:42,960 --> 00:03:49,050
So maybe the ultrasonic waves are going to bounce everywhere and come back to the ultrasonic sensor,

55
00:03:49,050 --> 00:03:52,000
which will read wrong data from time to time.

56
00:03:52,410 --> 00:03:59,700
OK, you may want to try in a bigger environment, for example, in the outside or just in a bigger

57
00:03:59,700 --> 00:04:02,120
room to try to see if you have the same problem.

58
00:04:02,340 --> 00:04:02,580
Right.

59
00:04:02,640 --> 00:04:06,600
So now you have a few of the reasons that maybe it's not working.

60
00:04:06,600 --> 00:04:10,470
If it's not working all right, if it's working correctly, you don't need to worry about it.

61
00:04:10,710 --> 00:04:13,770
But now what can you do if you are in the same situation as me?

62
00:04:13,770 --> 00:04:16,850
So you have correctly checked your how do I set up?

63
00:04:17,370 --> 00:04:22,080
Well, you just have one sensor so you can change it and you can change on the.

64
00:04:22,170 --> 00:04:28,740
And so I'm doing this on purpose so I can show you how to fix hardware with software.

65
00:04:29,100 --> 00:04:31,590
You can modify the software a little bit.

66
00:04:31,590 --> 00:04:36,690
And that's what we are going to do when we get the ultrasonic distance here.

67
00:04:36,960 --> 00:04:42,240
So we get the duration in Microcircuit of the Pulse and then we compute the distance.

68
00:04:42,510 --> 00:04:48,780
I'm going to apply a few filters here so we can regulate a little bit the values from the software.

69
00:04:48,780 --> 00:04:52,860
So this is not, of course, a one hundred percent solution to the problem.

70
00:04:53,040 --> 00:04:59,300
OK, but this is a nice fix that will allow us to continue and progress with this sensor and the circuit.

71
00:04:59,490 --> 00:04:59,700
So.

72
00:05:00,510 --> 00:05:08,820
What I'm going to do is I'm going to upload these programs activity 15, OK, where we put on a different

73
00:05:08,820 --> 00:05:11,300
energy from the distance we get.

74
00:05:11,550 --> 00:05:14,010
And also because here we print.

75
00:05:14,130 --> 00:05:18,060
OK, listen, make sure you have the same print along with distance.

76
00:05:18,960 --> 00:05:24,600
So I'm going to I can open the science monitor and you can see the different values we get.

77
00:05:24,660 --> 00:05:25,130
OK.

78
00:05:26,550 --> 00:05:33,330
So we have some values and you can see we have some weird values from time to time to get a better visualization

79
00:05:33,330 --> 00:05:33,540
here.

80
00:05:33,570 --> 00:05:36,390
I'm going to open the cell clutter, OK?

81
00:05:36,690 --> 00:05:38,280
And let's see, what do we get?

82
00:05:38,310 --> 00:05:44,700
So here you can see I am in 140, OK, this is the wall behind me.

83
00:05:45,040 --> 00:05:49,400
I'm going to put my hand and do some movement in front of the sensor.

84
00:05:49,710 --> 00:05:56,060
And you can see we OK, first thing we see is that we have some values that go way too high.

85
00:05:56,130 --> 00:06:03,030
OK, from time to time and the maximum value we could get here is 400 centimeters, OK, because this

86
00:06:03,030 --> 00:06:05,300
is the max range for the sensor.

87
00:06:06,390 --> 00:06:15,720
But you can see we get the value up to more than 3000 centimeters, which is well, which is definitely

88
00:06:15,720 --> 00:06:16,450
not correct.

89
00:06:16,890 --> 00:06:24,330
So the first thing maybe we can do is that anytime we get a value that is above four meters or 400 centimeters,

90
00:06:24,780 --> 00:06:27,420
well, we just don't take this value into account.

91
00:06:27,750 --> 00:06:34,860
So to do that, I'm going to create a new viable here that I'm going to call double previous.

92
00:06:36,800 --> 00:06:40,880
Distance and let's initialize it to 400.

93
00:06:43,340 --> 00:06:49,010
OK, and I'm going to go here, so I'm reading the duration and computing the distance, and I'm going

94
00:06:49,010 --> 00:06:52,700
to filter I'm going to add some filters here for the distance.

95
00:06:52,730 --> 00:06:53,570
Let's see if.

96
00:06:54,650 --> 00:06:55,850
Distance is greater.

97
00:06:56,950 --> 00:06:58,030
And 400.

98
00:07:00,640 --> 00:07:05,950
Then what I'm going to do is I'm not going to take this into account, I'm simply going to return to

99
00:07:06,610 --> 00:07:09,010
their previous distance.

100
00:07:09,010 --> 00:07:17,050
And if the distance is lower than 400, so if the distance is correct, I'm going to the previous distance

101
00:07:17,050 --> 00:07:20,710
is equal to the distance and then return the distance.

102
00:07:21,130 --> 00:07:23,530
So what this will do, let's let's make an example.

103
00:07:23,560 --> 00:07:25,450
So first, we have a pretty decent 400.

104
00:07:25,450 --> 00:07:27,570
We just initialized with a venue.

105
00:07:27,910 --> 00:07:29,880
So let's say we have a correct value here.

106
00:07:30,220 --> 00:07:31,120
So we get this done.

107
00:07:31,180 --> 00:07:32,620
Let's say one hundred forty.

108
00:07:33,460 --> 00:07:35,960
The distance is lower than four hundred.

109
00:07:36,190 --> 00:07:41,620
So now we said the previous distance, which become the distance to the previous distance, becomes

110
00:07:41,620 --> 00:07:43,120
one hundred and forty.

111
00:07:43,330 --> 00:07:46,030
OK, then we return one hundred and forty.

112
00:07:46,750 --> 00:07:53,030
The next thing we read, let's say we have a with the disease like three thousand so we get three thousand.

113
00:07:53,050 --> 00:07:54,450
Here we check that.

114
00:07:54,460 --> 00:07:57,020
OK, three thousand is actually greater than 400.

115
00:07:57,250 --> 00:08:04,060
So instead of using this value, we just return the previous one, the previous one, which is now one

116
00:08:04,060 --> 00:08:04,950
hundred forty.

117
00:08:05,320 --> 00:08:07,270
So let's upload this program here.

118
00:08:09,050 --> 00:08:18,950
And let's open the CIA again and you will see, OK, I get 140, you see, I still get many variations

119
00:08:18,950 --> 00:08:27,770
in the volume, OK, that I never get any value that is above one hundred forty or any value that is

120
00:08:27,770 --> 00:08:34,340
above actually 400, OK, because 400 is the maximum the cap for the sensor.

121
00:08:34,890 --> 00:08:41,390
So I can be sure by using this filter that I don't get any value above four hundred.

122
00:08:41,390 --> 00:08:43,850
So that's already a good first step.

123
00:08:44,240 --> 00:08:48,370
So first, eliminate any value that is outside of the range of the sensor.

124
00:08:48,770 --> 00:08:53,560
And no, I'm going to do another thing and this is called a complementary filter.

125
00:08:53,990 --> 00:08:55,820
What is a complementary filter?

126
00:08:56,240 --> 00:08:57,650
So a complementary filter.

127
00:08:57,650 --> 00:09:03,140
I'm not going to go into too many details here if you want to check that outside of the code.

128
00:09:03,170 --> 00:09:06,890
OK, this is a mathematical filter that we are going to use.

129
00:09:06,890 --> 00:09:12,950
And this is very often used to smooth out some data that you read from the sensor.

130
00:09:13,340 --> 00:09:20,860
So the complementary filter is actually quite simple, is instead of just returning the new distance,

131
00:09:20,870 --> 00:09:26,750
OK, we are going to return an average of the previous distance and a new distance.

132
00:09:27,050 --> 00:09:32,750
OK, let's let's actually write the code and then give some explanation to the new distance.

133
00:09:32,750 --> 00:09:39,620
We become previous distance, let's say, multiplied by zero point five.

134
00:09:40,160 --> 00:09:40,700
Let's.

135
00:09:41,760 --> 00:09:48,160
Distance multiplied by zero point five, but we can't the distance here.

136
00:09:48,270 --> 00:09:53,550
OK, we have the previous distance from the previous run of that function and then we do.

137
00:09:54,150 --> 00:09:57,250
So this is an example with zero point five and zero point five.

138
00:09:57,690 --> 00:10:01,290
So the distance becomes so the distance we have just measured.

139
00:10:01,740 --> 00:10:04,780
We take half of this value and half of the previous one.

140
00:10:05,340 --> 00:10:09,480
So to make a complementary filter, you simply have to store the previous value.

141
00:10:09,510 --> 00:10:14,390
So you have to, of course, the data at a certain frequency.

142
00:10:14,400 --> 00:10:21,470
So he attempts, OK, and so you get you still the previous distance to the previous data.

143
00:10:21,720 --> 00:10:26,430
And then when you get the new data, you make a mix of the previous data and the new data.

144
00:10:27,060 --> 00:10:33,630
So, for example, if you have just one spike, which is very high, this spike would actually be smoothed

145
00:10:34,110 --> 00:10:37,130
and you will not see so much weird behavior.

146
00:10:37,740 --> 00:10:42,610
So we can use the distance with zero point five and distance with zero point five.

147
00:10:42,960 --> 00:10:47,350
The actual important thing is that this and this equals to one.

148
00:10:47,400 --> 00:10:54,810
OK, so we could use, for example, the zero point seven and zero point three here and the bigger the

149
00:10:54,810 --> 00:10:56,910
number for the previous data.

150
00:10:57,310 --> 00:10:59,930
OK, the bigger the resistance to change.

151
00:11:00,240 --> 00:11:02,530
And actually, let's mix some examples.

152
00:11:02,550 --> 00:11:06,130
OK, I'm going to go with zero point five and zero point five.

153
00:11:06,390 --> 00:11:09,660
Let's upload that program so you can see just one additional line.

154
00:11:10,810 --> 00:11:18,750
And I'm going to happen to say a lot, and you can see so we still have some weird variations, OK,

155
00:11:18,940 --> 00:11:20,710
but not not that much.

156
00:11:21,150 --> 00:11:24,700
It's actually much better if you can see that the.

157
00:11:26,120 --> 00:11:28,280
Signal is a little bit smoother.

158
00:11:29,680 --> 00:11:37,750
All right, and now let's add, let's say here on nine and point one, which means that whenever we

159
00:11:37,750 --> 00:11:44,740
get to a new distance, we are going to keep 90 percent of the previous distance and only add 10 percent

160
00:11:44,740 --> 00:11:45,670
of the new distance.

161
00:11:45,980 --> 00:11:47,410
So he that's applaud.

162
00:11:48,590 --> 00:11:55,920
You will see that he's very smooth, but also in shape, put my hand here.

163
00:11:57,020 --> 00:12:02,050
It's very smooth, but also very low key to detect changes.

164
00:12:02,060 --> 00:12:03,160
I remove my hand.

165
00:12:03,710 --> 00:12:08,780
You can see it really takes some time to go through here to 140.

166
00:12:09,140 --> 00:12:18,520
I just put my hand again now and you can see it's really low, really slow, but it's much smoother.

167
00:12:19,250 --> 00:12:22,190
So now you have the formula for the complementary filter.

168
00:12:22,190 --> 00:12:26,060
And what you need to tinker is basically those two diameters.

169
00:12:26,270 --> 00:12:32,720
So the percentage, 90 percent, 10 percent here, make sure that the total is one hundred percent on

170
00:12:32,720 --> 00:12:33,620
just one.

171
00:12:34,040 --> 00:12:42,200
OK, so the bigger the coefficient for the data, the smoother the values you are going to read.

172
00:12:42,740 --> 00:12:43,490
But also this.

173
00:12:43,580 --> 00:12:46,340
The data will be updated a little bit late.

174
00:12:46,480 --> 00:12:52,640
OK, so if you need to be very reactive to what's happening in the environment, you don't want to just

175
00:12:52,640 --> 00:12:54,890
get the one of the three segments, OK?

176
00:12:54,890 --> 00:12:57,620
You want to get the warning as fast as you can.

177
00:12:57,650 --> 00:13:05,810
So you need to find a tradeoff between how smooth you want the data to be and how reactive you want

178
00:13:05,810 --> 00:13:09,030
to get the new available data.

179
00:13:09,380 --> 00:13:12,020
So you have two ways actually doing that.

180
00:13:12,020 --> 00:13:15,390
You can change the equation here and you can also increase the frequency.

181
00:13:15,410 --> 00:13:18,890
So if you read faster, this is going to be updated faster.

182
00:13:19,190 --> 00:13:22,570
OK, and instead of 100, I'm going to put 60.

183
00:13:22,850 --> 00:13:29,420
60 is the minimum value recommended by the manufacturer for this component.

184
00:13:29,780 --> 00:13:32,060
I'm going to go to 60 and not lower.

185
00:13:32,100 --> 00:13:36,050
OK, but with this, we are going to read the data first.

186
00:13:36,260 --> 00:13:42,490
And I'm going to use, let's see, 70 here and 30 percent.

187
00:13:43,280 --> 00:13:48,200
So every 60 milliseconds, I'm going to read the new data from the sensor, compute the distance and

188
00:13:48,200 --> 00:13:49,680
check that the distance is correct.

189
00:13:49,790 --> 00:13:54,040
If not, I wrote on the previous one and then apply a complementary filter.

190
00:13:54,480 --> 00:13:58,850
OK, 70 percent of the previous one, 30 percent of the new one.

191
00:13:59,750 --> 00:14:04,450
So let's applaud that and let's see what we get here.

192
00:14:05,420 --> 00:14:12,470
Let's open the CIA plotter and I'm going to put my hands in front.

193
00:14:13,350 --> 00:14:14,760
Of the SENSEOR.

194
00:14:16,170 --> 00:14:25,500
OK, and you can see now, OK, the data we read is much smoother and Andy here is actually going to

195
00:14:25,890 --> 00:14:29,420
be yellow now and I remove my hand and it's going to green.

196
00:14:29,760 --> 00:14:35,180
So it's already quite fast for us to measure the correct distance.

197
00:14:35,190 --> 00:14:36,930
I put my hand again yellow.

198
00:14:37,170 --> 00:14:38,980
I go closer and red.

199
00:14:39,270 --> 00:14:43,590
OK, so we have some accurate data, which is quite smooth.

200
00:14:43,700 --> 00:14:48,860
You can see on the cell plotter and it's also quite reactive.

201
00:14:49,560 --> 00:14:49,880
Great.

202
00:14:49,890 --> 00:14:56,400
So you have seen in this lesson how to actually fix the problem you have with the hardware by actually

203
00:14:56,400 --> 00:15:02,550
applying a few lines of software to filter the data you receive from the sensor.

204
00:15:02,900 --> 00:15:05,520
So this is, of course, a little bit more advanced.

205
00:15:05,940 --> 00:15:11,100
It's OK if you don't understand everything now, you might have to do some more research on the complementary

206
00:15:11,100 --> 00:15:16,080
filter and will know that we have our sensor working correctly.

207
00:15:16,770 --> 00:15:22,020
We can go to the next section and actually we will come back to DICENZO and we are going to use it a

208
00:15:22,020 --> 00:15:22,420
lot.

209
00:15:22,950 --> 00:15:25,340
Well, the final project of this class.

In the following lessons, you will walk with the LCD display screen components, right, you have just

2

00:00:09,690 --> 00:00:15,570
As you have seen with the sensor, you can get more interesting data here, the distance between the

4

sensor and an obstacle, a wall.

5
00:00:18,570 --> 00:00:24,440
And also, you could notice that distance was a little bit more complex to set up and to handle.

6

00:00:35,550 --> 00:00:38,600

This component is not in the stencils category.

11

12

13

# Section 17: IR Remote Controller - Make Your Projects More Interactive

1
00:00:00,180 --> 00:00:06,300
In the following lessons, you will learn how to control your Arduino application with an infrared remote

2
00:00:06,300 --> 00:00:14,030
control that you have now just finished the sections on the ultrasonic sensor and the LCD display screen,

3
00:00:14,460 --> 00:00:21,150
those two components, the first being a sensor and the signal being an actuator, allow you to create

4
00:00:21,150 --> 00:00:22,670
more complex applications.

5
00:00:23,100 --> 00:00:30,300
You can now get the distance from an obstacle or a wall and also add visual information with text,

6
00:00:30,450 --> 00:00:32,190
which is super useful.

7
00:00:32,760 --> 00:00:40,560
Now we are going to focus on the infrared remote control and the setup will contain an infrared receiver

8
00:00:40,800 --> 00:00:45,390
and a remote controller sending, of course, infrared signals.

9
00:00:46,070 --> 00:00:50,760
This controller is very similar to the controllers you use in everyday life.

10
00:00:50,760 --> 00:00:54,150
So we are going to use the remote controller from the starter kit.

11
00:00:54,390 --> 00:01:00,840
But later on, you will also be able to use other TV controller directly with your original application.

12
00:01:01,380 --> 00:01:07,140
During the following lessons, I will show you how to set up your Arduino secret and then you will discover

13
00:01:07,140 --> 00:01:10,510
how to get which button was pressed by the user.

14
00:01:11,040 --> 00:01:16,980
You will learn how to map buttons with your code to use any remote controller, and you will then be

15
00:01:16,980 --> 00:01:21,330
able to execute actions depending on the button which was pressed.

16
00:01:21,690 --> 00:01:27,070
This is really nice and we increase a lot the number of applications that you can create.

17
00:01:27,660 --> 00:01:29,210
Now let's get started.

00:00:09,060 --> 00:00:15,980
So when you have a sensor that gives you some values or photoresistor temperature sensor or put on Schmitter,

4
00:00:16,050 --> 00:00:23,910
a ultrasonic distance sensor, whatever, so it can be interesting to know not only what values you

5
00:00:23,910 --> 00:00:29,580

6
00:00:30,390 --> 00:00:36,360
So, for example, for the photoresistor sensor, you might put it somewhere and compute the average

7
00:00:36,420 --> 00:00:39,040
luminosity you get over 24 hours.

8
00:00:39,110 --> 00:00:43,890
OK, for example, of a one hour of whatever period of time you want.

9
00:00:44,100 --> 00:00:50,760
So to do that, what you need to do is to read some data from the sensor every X amount of time that

10
00:00:50,760 --> 00:00:55,180
you define and each reading, we are going to call that a sample.

11
00:00:55,200 --> 00:01:01,110
So you are going to save those samples inside an array and after a certain period of time, you are

12
00:01:01,110 --> 00:01:03,530
going to compute the average of these three.

13
00:01:03,900 --> 00:01:09,700
So this doing this can be very useful to do some measurements of your environment.

14
00:01:10,320 --> 00:01:15,690
So what you are going to do in this activity, you are going to get some simple so gets simpler.

OK, four, five, six.

00:01:27,670 --> 00:01:30,550
So actually, that's one hundred samples.

Of course, I'm not going to make the example for twenty four hours because the twenty four hour video

20

21
00:01:38,590 --> 00:01:42,730
But let's use this for one hundred samples that we take every 15 minutes.

22
00:01:43,210 --> 00:01:48,210
OK, and then those two parameters, you can just modify them as you want.

23
00:01:48,400 --> 00:01:53,970
And then so when you have those one hundred samples you are going to compute the average.

24
00:01:54,370 --> 00:01:57,610
So to compute the average, if you don't know the formula.

25
00:01:57,620 --> 00:01:58,920
The formula is very simple.

26
00:01:58,930 --> 00:02:05,410
You take the sum of all values and you divide that by the number of values.

27
00:02:05,590 --> 00:02:11,900
So for one hundred samples, you make the sum of all the elements in the array and you divide it by

28
00:02:12,040 --> 00:02:12,660
one hundred.

And so what you're going to do is you're going to print.

00:02:22,930 --> 00:02:23,730
and not delay.

32

And then after you get one hundred samples, you compute the average and you print print the average.

33
00:02:35,560 --> 00:02:41,500

# Section 19: Final project - Interactive Obstacle Detection

1
00:00:00,300 --> 00:00:03,460
It's now time to start the final project of the schools.

2
00:00:03,990 --> 00:00:08,610

00:00:08,880 --> 00:00:11,540

4

11
00:00:43,410 --> 00:00:47,960
So this project is an interactive obstacle detection application.

12
00:00:48,270 --> 00:00:52,740
So you have the ultrasonic sensor here, which would measure the distance in front of it.

13
00:00:53,010 --> 00:00:59,490
And you can see I also print the distance on the LCD display screen as well as increase.

14
00:00:59,520 --> 00:01:01,740

15

I increase the frequency, the frequency, the closer I am to the sensor.

00:01:08,490 --> 00:01:12,360

17
00:01:12,580 --> 00:01:13,920
I also have a warning.

18
00:01:14,130 --> 00:01:18,620
If I get too close, that's less than 50 centimeters.

19

And then if I get ready to close less than 10 centimeters, you can see we are in what I call the locked

20

21
00:01:26,650 --> 00:01:30,870
So the applications locked against the obstacle price to unlock here.

And then I need in order to unlock the application, I need to press here on the push button and now

26

the application is unlocked.

27
00:01:51,660 --> 00:01:56,410
And I can if I want, I can unlock it again by going to close to the sensor.

00:06:14,740 --> 00:06:20,720

00:00:35,190 --> 00:00:41,220

00:00:41,220 --> 00:00:44,490

00:00:48,510 --> 00:00:50,630

00:00:50,850 --> 00:00:57,810

15
00:00:58,200 --> 00:01:03,150
And then if you learn new things, new components, new knowledge, after this course, you can apply

16
00:01:03,150 --> 00:01:04,680
it also directly to the project.

17
00:01:05,520 --> 00:01:08,940
Here are a few ideas of what you can do to go further.

18
00:01:09,120 --> 00:01:11,880
Now you can add more settings.

19
00:01:11,880 --> 00:01:15,840
For example, do a reading all the blinking rates for the 80s.

20
00:01:16,170 --> 00:01:22,290
So with the infrared remote control, you get the so used to go between the settings, you save the

21
00:01:22,290 --> 00:01:29,250
settings to EPROM and then you add more screen modes on the LCD to display everything the program is

22
00:01:29,250 --> 00:01:29,940
going to be.

23
00:01:30,180 --> 00:01:35,550
So you can learn how to split and add in a program into different phases to better organize the code

24
00:01:35,760 --> 00:01:38,480
by functionality or by hardware component.

25
00:01:39,330 --> 00:01:41,730
You can learn how to use other hardware components.

26
00:01:41,730 --> 00:01:51,240
For example, a sensor which is a passive infrared sensor to detect human movement contrary to the ultrasonic

27
00:01:51,240 --> 00:01:54,920
sensor which measures the distance but paper.

28
00:01:55,170 --> 00:02:01,830
You can also detect if there is a human moving in front of the robot, you can add a buzzer to make

29
00:02:01,830 --> 00:02:06,040
a sound when the application is getting blocked because there is an obstacle.

30
00:02:06,330 --> 00:02:10,200
This will add more information and more interactiveness to the project.

31
00:02:11,040 --> 00:02:14,520
You can also make the unlocking functionality more complex.

32
00:02:14,820 --> 00:02:21,240
For example, with a four digit PIN code that you have to enter on the remote controller.

33
00:02:22,020 --> 00:02:29,190
Then you can build a mobile robot and use parts of this application to make the robot move safely in

34
00:02:29,190 --> 00:02:30,240
its environment.

35
00:02:31,140 --> 00:02:37,410
You can also transform the application into a security app to monitor when someone is crossing a door,

36
00:02:37,410 --> 00:02:39,930
for example, and etc..

37
00:02:40,230 --> 00:02:43,830
Use your imagination to build anything you want.
